- **Conversion Trick:** Any UDC can be made prefix-free by the "comma insertion" trick (use a special termination symbol) without changing the code alphabet.
- **Memoryless Source Assumption:** All derivations in this module assume a DMS (discrete memoryless source) — symbol probabilities are independent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kraft Inequality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kraft Inequality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Kraft Inequality** (also known as the **Kraft–McMillan Inequality**) is a fundamental necessary and sufficient condition in information theory that governs the existence of a **prefix-free binary code** for a given set of source symbols with specified codeword lengths. For a discrete source with an alphabet of size $n$ and codeword lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ (where each $l_i$ is a positive integer), the Kraft Inequality states that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The Kraft Inequality is the *theoretical gateway* to source coding. Without satisfying this condition, **no prefix code can be constructed**, regardless of the cleverness of the coding algorithm. It is the mathematical license that permits the construction of an instantaneous (prefix) code.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mailbox Analogy"

Imagine a giant **post office with $2^L$ identical mailboxes** arranged in $L$ rows deep (rows numbered 0, 1, 2, ..., $L$). A codeword of length $l_i$ is like a customer who, at each level of a binary decision tree (left = 0, right = 1), chooses one branch. A length-$l_i$ codeword will, in the future, "occupy" or "block" $2^{L - l_i}$ mailboxes at the deepest level because once a codeword ends, every possible extension of it (appending 0s and 1s) is **forbidden** to other codewords.

- If the total number of mailboxes that all codewords attempt to **block** exceeds the $2^L$ available, the system is over-subscribed — and a prefix code **cannot exist**.
- The Kraft Inequality $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ is precisely the mathematical check that the "blocking demand" never exceeds available capacity.

## 1.3 Why is it called "Kraft" and "McMillan"?

- **Leonard Kraft (1949)** proved the inequality for **prefix codes** (one direction — necessity).
- **Brockway McMillan (1956)** proved the *reverse direction* — that the condition is also **sufficient** for uniquely decodable codes.

Hence the full name **Kraft–McMillan Inequality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Suffix "$-l_i$" in the exponent:** The exponent $-l_i$ is **negative** because longer codewords consume *fewer* leaf nodes (a small price), while shorter codewords consume *more* leaf nodes (a large price). The inequality thus penalizes short codewords appropriately.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Theorem

The Kraft Inequality is built upon the **binary decision tree** model. We dissect it as follows:

- **Step 1 — Tree Construction:** Construct a complete binary tree of depth $L = \max(l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n)$. This tree has exactly $2^L$ leaves at depth $L$.
- **Step 2 — Codeword as a Path:** Each codeword of length $l_i$ corresponds to a node at depth $l_i$. In a prefix code, **no two codewords can lie on the same root-to-leaf path** — i.e., no codeword is an ancestor of another.
- **Step 3 — "Blocking" Effect:** A codeword sitting at depth $l_i$ blocks exactly $2^{L - l_i}$ leaves at depth $L$ (all possible extensions of the codeword).
- **Step 4 — Capacity Argument:** Since each codeword independently blocks a disjoint set of leaves, the total number of leaves blocked cannot exceed $2^L$.
- **Step 5 — Divide by $2^L$:** This yields $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{L - l_i} \leq 2^L$, which simplifies to $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$.

## 2.2 Sufficiency — Why $\leq 1$ is Enough

> [!TIP]
> The converse is the more powerful (and exam-favorite) direction. If $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$, then a **prefix code with these exact lengths CAN be constructed**. The constructive proof is by the **Huffman / tree-greedy algorithm**, which the KTU examiner often asks as a 14-mark question.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Logical Step | Engineering Intuition |
|---|---|
| Build tree of depth $L$ | Establishes a finite, well-defined "address space" of $2^L$ slots. |
| Codeword occupies $2^{L-l_i}$ leaves | A short codeword has many descendants — all are unusable by others. |
| Disjointness (prefix property) | Two prefix codewords can never share a descendant subtree. |
| Capacity $\leq 2^L$ | Tree leaves are a physical resource; total consumption $\leq$ total supply. |
| Divide by $2^L$ | Normalize to obtain the elegant dimensionless inequality. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory formulas to memorize for the KTU 2024 University Exam (Module 1)**

| Formula | Statement | Condition / When Used |
|---|---|---|
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | Kraft Inequality (prefix codes) | Necessary & sufficient for binary prefix code existence |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | Kraft Inequality (D-ary codes) | For alphabet of size $D$ (e.g., ternary $D=3$) |
| $l_i = \lceil -\log_2 p_i \rceil$ | Shannon codeword length | For near-optimal length assignment |
| $H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$ | Source coding theorem | Average codeword length bounds |
| $2^{L - l_i}$ | Leaves "blocked" by codeword $i$ | In the tree-construction proof |
| $\eta = \dfrac{H(S)}{\bar{L}}$ | Coding efficiency | Ratio of entropy to average length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Kraft Inequality is the foundation upon which every practical lossless compression algorithm (Huffman, Shannon–Fano, Arithmetic coding) is justified. It guarantees that the lengths we *assign* are actually *realizable* as a valid prefix code.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Necessity ($\Rightarrow$): A Prefix Code IMPLIES the Inequality Holds

> **Given:** A prefix code with lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$.
> **To Prove:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$.

**Step 1 —** Let $L = \max(l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n)$. Consider a complete binary tree of depth $L$. The total number of leaves at depth $L$ is:

$$N_{\text{total}} = 2^L$$

**Step 2 —** For any codeword $c_i$ of length $l_i$, all its descendants at depth $L$ are *forbidden* to be other codewords (because of the prefix property). The number of descendants at depth $L$ is:

$$N_i = 2^{L - l_i}$$

**Step 3 —** Because the code is prefix-free, the descendants of different codewords are **disjoint sets**. Therefore, the sum of descendants over all codewords cannot exceed the total number of leaves:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{L - l_i} \leq 2^L$$

**Step 4 —** Divide both sides by $2^L$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

This completes the **necessity** proof. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sufficiency ($\Leftarrow$): The Inequality IMPLIES a Prefix Code Exists

> **Given:** A set of positive integers $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ satisfying $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$.
> **To Prove:** A prefix code with these exact lengths exists.

**Step 1 —** Without loss of generality, sort the lengths: $l_1 \leq l_2 \leq \ldots \leq l_n$. (Sorting is allowed; it just relabels symbols.)

**Step 2 —** Initialize a counter $\mathcal{C} = 0$ representing the *number of leaves already allocated* (normalized).

**Step 3 —** For each $i = 1$ to $n$, assign the codeword $c_i$ to occupy the subtree representing the **next available** $2^{L - l_i}$ leaves. Equivalently, in the tree, navigate greedily to the first available node at depth $l_i$.

**Step 4 —** After assigning $c_i$, update the counter: $\mathcal{C} \leftarrow \mathcal{C} + 2^{-l_i}$.

**Step 5 —** By the induction hypothesis and the given inequality, after assigning all $n$ codewords:

$$\mathcal{C} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

Since the counter never exceeds 1, an available slot always exists. Hence, the construction succeeds, and a prefix code exists. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A source has 4 symbols with codeword lengths $l_1 = 1, l_2 = 2, l_3 = 3, l_3 = 4$. Check whether a prefix code can be constructed.

**Solution:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4}$$

We evaluate term-by-term:

$$= 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.0625 = 0.9375 \leq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Since $0.9375 \leq 1$, a **prefix code exists**. One valid assignment is: $\{0, 10, 110, 1110\}$.

## 3.4 Counter-Example (When the Inequality Fails)

> **Problem:** Check if a prefix code with lengths $\{1, 1, 1\}$ is possible.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} 2^{-1} = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.5 > 1 \quad \times$$

Since $1.5 > 1$, **no prefix code exists** with three codewords all of length 1. This is intuitive: there are only 2 binary strings of length 1, and we need 3 distinct codewords.

## 3.5 Worked Problem — D-ary Kraft Inequality

> **Problem:** For a ternary (D = 3) code, lengths are $l_1 = 1, l_2 = 2, l_3 = 2$. Check feasibility.

**Solution:** The D-ary Kraft Inequality is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} 3^{-l_i} = 3^{-1} + 3^{-2} + 3^{-2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{3 + 1 + 1}{9} = \frac{5}{9} \approx 0.556 \leq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Hence, a **ternary prefix code exists**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binary Tree Blockage Diagram (Mermaid)

The following diagram visualizes how codewords of lengths $\{2, 2, 3\}$ consume the leaf space of a binary tree of depth $L = 3$:

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["ROOT (Depth 0)"] --> n0["0"]
    root --> n1["1"]
    n0 --> n00["00"]
    n0 --> n01["01"]
    n1["1"] --> n10["10"]
    n1 --> n11["11"]
    n00["00 (Depth 2)"] --> n000["000 (DEPTH 3)"]
    n00 --> n001["001 (DEPTH 3)"]
    n01["01 (Depth 2)"] --> n010["010 (DEPTH 3)"]
    n01 --> n011["011 (DEPTH 3)"]
    n10["10 (Depth 2)"] --> n100["100 (DEPTH 3)"]
    n10 --> n101["101 (DEPTH 3)"]
    n11["11 (Depth 2)"] --> n110["110 (DEPTH 3)"]
    n11 --> n111["111 (DEPTH 3)"]
    cA["CODEWORD A = 00 (L=2) BLOCKS 000, 001"] -.-> n000
    cA -.-> n001
    cB["CODEWORD B = 10 (L=2) BLOCKS 100, 101"] -.-> n100
    cB -.-> n101
    cC["CODEWORD C = 110 (L=3) OCCUPIES 110"] -.-> n110
    classDef codeword fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef blocked fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class cA,cB,cC codeword
    class n000,n001,n100,n101,n110 blocked
    class n010,n011,n111 free
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Yellow nodes (CODEWORD A, B, C):** Actual codewords of the prefix code.
- **Red leaves:** Descendants "blocked" by codewords A and B (forbidden to other codewords).
- **Green leaves (010, 011, 111):** Unused, free for future extensions.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Construction of a Prefix Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Set of codeword lengths l1, l2, ..., ln"] --> B["STEP 1: Verify Kraft Inequality Sum 2 POWER neg li less than equal 1"]
    B --> C{"Inequality Satisfied?"}
    C -->|NO| D["OUTPUT: NO PREFIX CODE EXISTS. Adjust lengths."]
    C -->|YES| E["STEP 2: Construct Binary Tree of Depth L = max li"]
    E --> F["STEP 3: Sort lengths in non-decreasing order"]
    F --> G["STEP 4: Greedy Assignment - assign shortest available path to each codeword"]
    G --> H["STEP 5: Verify prefix property - no codeword is prefix of another"]
    H --> I["OUTPUT: VALID PREFIX CODE c1, c2, ..., cn"]
    D --> A
    classDef inputNode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef decisionNode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef errorNode fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef outputNode fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    class A inputNode
    class C decisionNode
    class B,E,F,G,H processNode
    class D errorNode
    class I outputNode
```

## 4.3 Kraft Sum Visualization Bar Chart

```mermaid
graph LR
    P["KRAFT SUM: sum of 2 POWER neg li"] --> Q["If sum less than 1: SPARE CAPACITY remains"]
    P --> R["If sum EQUALS 1: PERFECTLY TIGHT, no room to spare"]
    P --> S["If sum GREATER than 1: OVER-SUBSCRIBED, code impossible"]
    classDef ok fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tight fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    class Q ok
    class R tight
    class S bad
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

### Q1. State the Kraft Inequality. Under what condition is it both necessary and sufficient?

**Model Answer:**

The Kraft Inequality states that for a set of codeword lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ of a binary prefix code, the following must hold:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

It is **necessary** because any valid prefix code must satisfy it (tree-blockage argument), and **sufficient** because if the inequality holds, a prefix code can be explicitly constructed using a greedy tree-assignment procedure. For uniquely decodable codes, the same condition is necessary and sufficient (McMillan's extension).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Is the Kraft Inequality satisfied for codeword lengths $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$? Justify.

**Model Answer:**

Computing the Kraft sum:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} = 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

Since $0.9375 \leq 1$, the **Kraft Inequality is satisfied**, and a prefix code exists. One such code: $\{0, 10, 110, 1110\}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Evaluation – Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the Kraft Inequality for a binary prefix code. Clearly show both the necessity and sufficiency directions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A discrete memoryless source has 5 symbols with probabilities:
$$p(s_1) = 0.4, \; p(s_2) = 0.2, \; p(s_3) = 0.2, \; p(s_4) = 0.1, \; p(s_5) = 0.1$$
Using the Shannon–Fano procedure, determine codeword lengths and verify the Kraft Inequality.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Statement and Proof [7 Marks]

**Statement:** For a binary prefix code with codeword lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$, the Kraft Inequality states:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

**Necessity Proof (2 Marks):** Let $L = \max(l_i)$. Consider a complete binary tree of depth $L$ with $2^L$ leaves. Each codeword of length $l_i$ blocks $2^{L - l_i}$ leaves at depth $L$ (all extensions). By the prefix property, blocks are disjoint:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{L - l_i} \leq 2^L \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

**Sufficiency Proof (3 Marks):** Sort lengths ascendingly. Greedily assign codewords to the first available subtree at the required depth. After each assignment, the cumulative Kraft sum increases by $2^{-l_i}$. Since the final sum is $\leq 1$ by hypothesis, the cumulative sum never exceeds 1, so an available slot always exists. Thus a valid prefix code can be constructed.

**Engineering Significance (2 Marks):** The inequality is the foundation of all lossless source coding — it guarantees that any length assignment satisfying the condition is realizable, enabling optimal codes like Huffman.

---

#### Part (b) — Shannon–Fano Length Assignment and Kraft Verification [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Probabilities (1 Mark):**

| Symbol | $p(s_i)$ | $-\log_2 p(s_i)$ | $l_i = \lceil -\log_2 p(s_i) \rceil$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0.4 | 1.3219 | 2 |
| $s_2$ | 0.2 | 2.3219 | 3 |
| $s_3$ | 0.2 | 2.3219 | 3 |
| $s_4$ | 0.1 | 3.3219 | 4 |
| $s_5$ | 0.1 | 3.3219 | 4 |

**Step 2 — Kraft Sum Verification (3 Marks):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-4}$$

$$= 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 = 0.625 \leq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Valid Code Construction (2 Marks):** A valid prefix code is:
- $s_1 \to 00$
- $s_2 \to 010$
- $s_3 \to 011$
- $s_4 \to 1000$
- $s_5 \to 1001$

**Step 4 — Average Length (1 Mark):**

$$\bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i = (0.4)(2) + (0.2)(3) + (0.2)(3) + (0.1)(4) + (0.1)(4)$$

$$= 0.8 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.4 + 0.4 = 2.8 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

The entropy is $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i = 2.122$ bits/symbol. Since $H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$, the source coding theorem is satisfied.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a clear binary-tree diagram, explain how the Kraft Inequality arises from the "leaf-blocking" argument. Use $L = 3$ and lengths $\{2, 2, 3\}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a source with probabilities $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$, design a Huffman code, compute the average codeword length, and verify the Kraft Inequality.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Tree-Blocking Argument [7 Marks]

**Setup (1 Mark):** Let $L = 3$ (deepest codeword). The tree has $2^3 = 8$ leaves at depth 3: $\{000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111\}$.

**Codeword A of length 2 (2 Marks):** Assigns path "00". All descendants at depth 3 are blocked: $\{000, 001\}$. Count: $2^{3-2} = 2$ leaves blocked.

**Codeword B of length 2 (2 Marks):** Assigns path "10". All descendants at depth 3 are blocked: $\{100, 101\}$. Count: $2^{3-2} = 2$ leaves blocked.

**Codeword C of length 3 (1 Mark):** Assigns path "110". Itself is a leaf at depth 3. Count: $2^{3-3} = 1$ leaf.

**Kraft Inequality (1 Mark):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.125 = 0.625 \leq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Total leaves blocked = $2 + 2 + 1 = 5 \leq 8 = 2^L$. Consistent.

---

#### Part (b) — Huffman Code Design and Verification [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Sort Probabilities (1 Mark):** $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$

**Step 2 — Huffman Tree Construction (3 Marks):**

- Combine the two lowest: $0.125 + 0.125 = 0.25$. New list: $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.25\}$.
- Combine the two lowest: $0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50$. New list: $\{0.5, 0.5\}$.
- Combine: $0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0$. Root reached.

**Step 3 — Codeword Assignment (1 Mark):**

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0.5 | 0 | 1 |
| $s_2$ | 0.25 | 10 | 2 |
| $s_3$ | 0.125 | 110 | 3 |
| $s_4$ | 0.125 | 111 | 3 |

**Step 4 — Kraft Verification (1 Mark):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} = 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.0 \leq 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Average Length (1 Mark):**

$$\bar{L} = (0.5)(1) + (0.25)(2) + (0.125)(3) + (0.125)(3) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

Entropy: $H(S) = 1.75$ bits/symbol. Hence $\bar{L} = H(S)$, and efficiency $\eta = 100\%$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the negation of the exponent:** Students often write $2^{l_i}$ instead of $2^{-l_i}$. The exponent is **negative** — memorize this.
> 2. **Using $\leq$ vs $=$ confusion:** The inequality is $\leq 1$, not $< 1$. Equality is allowed (Huffman codes often achieve equality).
> 3. **Not sorting lengths:** The sufficiency proof requires sorted lengths; the inequality itself does not, but for construction, order matters.
> 4. **Confusing prefix codes with uniquely decodable codes:** The Kraft inequality applies to *both*, but the proof structure differs (Kraft = prefix; McMillan = UD).
> 5. **Arithmetic mistakes in long sums:** Always compute powers of 2 explicitly: $2^{-1} = 0.5, 2^{-2} = 0.25, 2^{-3} = 0.125, 2^{-4} = 0.0625, 2^{-5} = 0.03125$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Kraft Inequality (Module 1)**

- 🔹 **Kraft Inequality** is the **necessary and sufficient condition** for the existence of a binary prefix code with lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$.
- 🔹 **Core formula:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ (binary), $\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1$ (D-ary).
- 🔹 **Exponent is negative** — longer codewords contribute smaller terms to the sum.
- 🔹 **Tree-blockage interpretation:** Length-$l_i$ codeword blocks $2^{L - l_i}$ leaves at depth $L$.
- 🔹 **Equality is allowed** — Huffman codes typically achieve equality ($\sum 2^{-l_i} = 1$).
- 🔹 **Sorting lengths** is required for the constructive (sufficiency) proof.
- 🔹 **Average length** $\bar{L}$ satisfies $H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$ (Shannon's noiseless coding theorem).
- 🔹 **Standard powers to memorize:** $2^{-1}=0.5$, $2^{-2}=0.25$, $2^{-3}=0.125$, $2^{-4}=0.0625$, $2^{-5}=0.03125$, $2^{-6}=0.015625$.
- 🔹 **Engineering applications:** JPEG (Huffman), MP3, ZIP, PNG, PDF, FAX — all use prefix codes validated by Kraft.
- 🔹 **Distinguish Kraft (1949) from McMillan (1956):** Kraft proved necessity for prefix codes; McMillan extended sufficiency to uniquely decodable codes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Huffman Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Huffman Code # Huffman Code — KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Study Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Huffman Code (David A. Huffman, 1952):** An optimal, statistically lossless, **prefix-free variable-length source code** that maps source symbols to binary codewords such that symbols with higher probabilities of occurrence are assigned shorter codewords, and symbols with lower probabilities are assigned longer codewords. The result is a code that minimizes the **expected (average) codeword length** $\bar{L}$ of the source, subject to the prefix condition (Kraft inequality: $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$). In formal notation, for a discrete memoryless source (DMS) with alphabet $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_q\}$ and probability mass function $P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_q\}$, the Huffman code produces a codeword length set $\{l_1, l_2, \dots, l_q\}$ such that: $$\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{q} p_i \, l_i \;\;\text{is minimized}$$ subject to the prefix constraint. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Express Lane" Supermarket Imagine a supermarket with a single billing counter: - **Busy customers (high probability items)** like milk and bread — the cashier knows their prices by heart and **whisks them through in seconds** (short codewords). - **Rare customers (low probability items)** like a 50-year aged cheese — the cashier **stops, looks up the price book, and keys in multiple digits** (long codewords). A naive cashier would type the *full* price code for *every* item, even for the common ones. **Huffman coding does exactly the reverse** — it gives common items a "shortcut code" and rare items a "long code." The overall time (bits transmitted) goes **way down**, but no information is ever lost (lossless). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Prefix-Free"?** No codeword is allowed to be the *starting prefix* of another codeword. This guarantees **instant, unambiguous decoding** without waiting for future symbols — the decoder recognizes a codeword the moment the bits uniquely match. This is also why Morse code in early telegraphy failed: 'E' (`.`) is a prefix of 'A' (`.−`) and 'T' (`−`), leading to severe ambiguity. ### 1.3 KTU Syllabus Position & CO Alignment | Parameter | Value | | :--- | :--- | | **Course Outcome (CO)** | CO1 — Apply source coding and channel coding techniques to compute efficiency, redundancy, and capacity. | | **Bloom's Level** | Apply / Analyze (L3–L4) | | **Module** | Module 1 — Source Coding | | **Prerequisite** | Entropy, Shannon's source coding theorem, Kraft–McMillan inequality | | **Marks Weightage (Typical ESE)** | 7 to 14 marks (frequent Part B question) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why Huffman Works The genius of Huffman coding is grounded in two information-theoretic pillars: 1. **Shannon's Source Coding Theorem:** The average codeword length $\bar{L}$ cannot be reduced below the source entropy $H(S)$. 2. **Kraft's Inequality (prefix constraint):** A prefix code exists **iff** $\sum_{i=1}^{q} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$. Huffman's algorithm constructs a code tree by **repeatedly combining the two least-probable symbols** into a single composite "super-symbol." This greedy bottom-up merging guarantees the prefix property while driving $\bar{L}$ to its theoretical minimum. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic 1. **List** all source symbols $s_i$ with their probabilities $p_i$ in **descending** order. 2. **Combine** the two symbols with the **smallest probabilities** into a single auxiliary node whose probability equals the **sum** of the two. 3. **Re-sort** the reduced list (now $q-1$ symbols) in descending order. 4. **Repeat** Steps 2–3 until only **one** composite node remains (with total probability 1.0). 5. **Assign bits:** starting from the root, label every left branch '0' and every right branch '1' (or vice versa). Read each symbol's codeword by traversing from root to leaf. > [!TIP] > **The "Combine-the-Two-Smallest" Golden Rule:** Always merge the two lowest probabilities *at the current stage* — not the globally lowest from the start. Many KTU valuation errors occur when students forget to re-sort after each merge. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Application | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Average Code Length** | $\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{q} p_i \, l_i$ | $l_i$ = codeword length of $s_i$ | Direct numerical evaluation (3-mark problems) | | 2 | **Source Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{q} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | Lower bound on $\bar{L}$ | | 3 | **Coding Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\,\%$ | always $\eta \leq 1$ | Mandatory in every Part B question | | 4 | **Redundancy** | $R = 1 - \eta$ | fractional waste | Often asked in 3-mark questions | | 5 | **Kraft Inequality (Prefix)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{q} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | equality for *full* trees | Verify a code is prefix-free | | 6 | **Variance of Code Length** | $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i (l_i - \bar{L})^2$ | spread measure | Sometimes asked to check "goodness" | | 7 | **Shannon's Bound** | $H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$ | tight asymptotic | Justifies optimality of Huffman | | 8 | **Huffman Optimality** | $\bar{L}_{\min}$ unique except for ties | greedy optimality | Common in KTU theory questions | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical bar `\|` inside any table cell above.** I have used $\bar{L}$, $2^{-l_i}$ etc. to keep the markdown table parser safe. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Huffman coding is *not* a textbook curiosity — it is the silent engine inside: - **ZIP / GZIP archives** (DEFLATE = LZ77 + Huffman). - **JPEG image compression** (entropy stage of the codec). - **MP3 audio** (residual signal is Huffman-coded). - **PNG images** (uses Deflate/Huffman). - **Fax machines (T.4 / T.6 standards)** — Group 3 fax uses a 1-D Huffman variant. - **Network protocols** — RTP payload formats, BLE advertising packets. - **Embedded firmware** — microcontroller bootloaders (e.g., TI MSP430) use Huffman tables to compress update images. > In every application, the trade-off is the **same**: spend a small CPU cost in exchange for **30–90 % bit-rate reduction** with **zero information loss**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Standard Huffman Construction (14-Mark Pattern) **Problem:** A source emits five symbols with probabilities as given. Construct the Huffman code, find the average codeword length, entropy, efficiency, and redundancy. | Symbol | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $s_4$ | $s_5$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Probability | 0.35 | 0.25 | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.05 | #### Step 1 — Initial Sorted List (descending) $$P^{(0)} = \{0.35,\ 0.25,\ 0.20,\ 0.15,\ 0.05\}$$ #### Step 2 — Merge the Two Smallest (0.15 and 0.05) Create a composite node with probability $0.15 + 0.05 = 0.20$. $$P^{(1)} = \{0.35,\ 0.25,\ \underline{0.20^*},\ 0.20\}$$ > Note: We now have **two** nodes with probability 0.20. One is the *original* $s_3$, the other is the *new composite* node $C_1$ combining $s_4$ and $s_5$. This tie-handling is what KTU examiners love to test. #### Step 3 — Re-Sort & Merge the Two Smallest (0.20 and 0.20) Combine the two 0.20 nodes into $C_2$ with probability $0.40$. $$P^{(2)} = \{0.40,\ 0.35,\ 0.25\}$$ #### Step 4 — Merge the Two Smallest (0.25 and 0.35) Wait — we must merge the two **smallest**. So merge 0.25 and 0.35 into $C_3$ with probability $0.60$. $$P^{(3)} = \{0.60,\ 0.40\}$$ #### Step 5 — Final Merge (0.60 and 0.40) Root node with probability $1.00$. Tree is complete. #### Step 6 — Tree & Code Assignment | Symbol | Probability | Codeword (from tree) | Length $l_i$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $s_1$ | 0.35 | 11 | 2 | | $s_2$ | 0.25 | 10 | 2 | | $s_3$ | 0.20 | 01 | 2 | | $s_4$ | 0.15 | 000 | 3 | | $s_5$ | 0.05 | 001 | 3 | #### Step 7 — Average Code Length $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{L} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \, l_i \\ &= (0.35)(2) + (0.25)(2) + (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.05)(3) \\ &= 0.70 + 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.15 \\ &= 2.20 \;\text{bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 8 — Source Entropy $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \log_2 p_i \\ &= -[0.35\log_2 0.35 + 0.25\log_2 0.25 + 0.20\log_2 0.20 + 0.15\log_2 0.15 + 0.05\log_2 0.05] \\ &\approx -[0.35(-1.5146) + 0.25(-2.0000) + 0.20(-2.3219) + 0.15(-2.7370) + 0.05(-4.3219)] \\ &\approx 0.5301 + 0.5000 + 0.4644 + 0.4105 + 0.2161 \\ &\approx 2.1211 \;\text{bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 9 — Efficiency and Redundancy $$ \eta = \frac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\,\% = \frac{2.1211}{2.20} \times 100\,\% \approx 96.41\,\% $$ $$ R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9641 = 0.0359 \approx 3.59\,\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity check via Kraft Inequality:** > $\sum 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.0 \leq 1$ ✔️ — The code is a **complete** (full) prefix tree. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Tie-Breaking & Variance Suppose a second Huffman tree (alternate tie break) gives: | Symbol | Probability | Codeword (Alt) | Length | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $s_1$ | 0.35 | 00 | 2 | | $s_2$ | 0.25 | 01 | 2 | | $s_3$ | 0.20 | 10 | 2 | | $s_4$ | 0.15 | 110 | 3 | | $s_5$ | 0.05 | 111 | 3 | Both trees yield the **same** $\bar{L} = 2.20$ bits/symbol but the **length distribution is identical**. Hence the **variance is also identical** (Huffman is optimal — and in this case *uniquely* length-optimal): $$ \sigma^2 = \sum p_i (l_i - \bar{L})^2 = 3(0.20)(0.2)^2 + 2(0.05)(0.8)^2 \text{ after re-distribution} $$ > [!NOTE] > **General Theorem:** The Huffman code is **length-unique** (the set of lengths is unique) even if the actual codewords differ due to tie-breaking. This is a frequent 3-mark KTU theory question. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Extended Huffman (Second-Order Extension) For block coding of two symbols at a time, the *joint probability* $p_{ij} = p_i p_j$ is used, giving $\bar{L}_2$ bits per **pair**, so the per-symbol length is $\bar{L}_2/2$. As block size $n \to \infty$, this ratio approaches $H(S)$ — Shannon's source coding theorem in action. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Huffman Tree Construction Block Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["Start: List q symbols with probabilities"] --> B["Sort in descending order"] B --> C{"More than 1 node
remaining?"} C -- "Yes" --> D["Pick two smallest
probabilities p_a and p_b"] D --> E["Create parent node
with p = p_a + p_b"] E --> F["Re-sort the reduced list"] F --> C C -- "No" --> G["Root reached.
Assign 0 to left, 1 to right"] G --> H["Read codewords from
root to each leaf"] H --> I["Output: Prefix-free
optimal codebook"] ``` ### 4.2 Huffman Tree for Worked Example 1 ```mermaid graph TD R["ROOT (P=1.00)"] R --> N1["P=0.60
0"] R --> N2["P=0.40
1"] N1 --> N3["P=0.35 = s1
0"] N1 --> N4["P=0.25 = s2
1"] N2 --> N5["P=0.20 = s3
0"] N2 --> N6["P=0.20 = C1
1"] N6 --> N7["P=0.15 = s4
0"] N6 --> N8["P=0.05 = s5
1"] ``` **Reading the codes (left=0, right=1):** - s1 → 00 (length 2) - s2 → 01 (length 2) - s3 → 10 (length 2) - s4 → 110 (length 3) - s5 → 111 (length 3) ### 4.3 Encoder / Decoder Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A["Source
Symbols"] --> B["Symbol
Buffer"] B --> C["Probability
Model"] C --> D["Huffman
Tree Lookup"] D --> E["Bitstream
Output"] E --> F["Channel /
Storage"] F --> G["Bitstream
Input"] G --> H["Huffman
Tree Lookup"] H --> I["Decoded
Symbols"] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** When asked to draw the "block diagram of source coding system," include the **probability estimator** — many students forget this and lose 1–2 marks. Both encoder and decoder must use the **same** tree (transmitted once as a side-channel header, typically DEFLATE-style). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State and explain the prefix condition for a code. Why is it necessary for instantaneous decoding?** **Model Answer:** A code satisfies the **prefix condition** if **no codeword is a prefix of any other codeword** in the codebook. Equivalently, $\sum_{i=1}^{q} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ (Kraft's inequality). It is necessary for **instantaneous decoding** because the receiver can identify a symbol the moment its full codeword arrives at the leaf of the code tree — it does not have to peek at future bits. This avoids buffering delay and ambiguity, unlike comma-coded or non-prefix schemes. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only "no codeword is a prefix of another" without the **Kraft inequality** statement. Both forms are expected; you **lose 1 mark** for missing the mathematical condition. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Why is the Huffman code called "optimal" among all prefix codes? Mention the bound it satisfies.** **Model Answer:** Huffman code is called *optimal* because, for a given discrete memoryless source, its **greedy merging procedure** (combining the two lowest-probability symbols at each step) produces a prefix code that **minimizes the expected codeword length** $\bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i$. It satisfies Shannon's source coding bound: $$H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$$ i.e., the average length never exceeds entropy by more than 1 bit per symbol, and asymptotically (via $n$-block extension) $\bar{L} \to H(S)$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- **Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** A source has six symbols with probabilities $\{0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.10, 0.10, 0.05\}$. (i) Construct the Huffman code. (ii) Find the average codeword length and coding efficiency. (7 marks) **(b)** For the above source, verify the **Kraft inequality** and compute the **redundancy**. Discuss why Huffman code is length-unique even if multiple valid code trees exist. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 — Sort:** $\{0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.10, 0.10, 0.05\}$ **Step 2 — Merge 0.05 and 0.10:** New node = 0.15. List: $\{0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10\}$ **Step 3 — Merge 0.10 and 0.15:** New node = 0.25. List: $\{0.30, 0.25^*, 0.25^*, 0.20\}$ **Step 4 — Merge 0.20 and 0.25 (smaller pair):** New node = 0.45. List: $\{0.45, 0.30, 0.25\}$ **Step 5 — Merge 0.25 and 0.30:** New node = 0.55. List: $\{0.55, 0.45\}$ **Step 6 — Merge 0.55 and 0.45 → Root = 1.0** | Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $s_1$ | 0.30 | 11 | 2 | | $s_2$ | 0.25 | 10 | 2 | | $s_3$ | 0.20 | 01 | 2 | | $s_4$ | 0.10 | 000 | 3 | | $s_5$ | 0.10 | 0011 | 4 | | $s_6$ | 0.05 | 0010 | 4 | **Average length:** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{L} &= (0.30)(2)+(0.25)(2)+(0.20)(2)+(0.10)(3)+(0.10)(4)+(0.05)(4) \\ &= 0.60+0.50+0.40+0.30+0.40+0.20 = 2.40 \;\text{bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ **Entropy:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -[0.30\log_2 0.30 + 0.25\log_2 0.25 + 0.20\log_2 0.20 \\ &\quad + 0.10\log_2 0.10 + 0.10\log_2 0.10 + 0.05\log_2 0.05] \\ &\approx 0.5211 + 0.5000 + 0.4644 + 0.3322 + 0.3322 + 0.2161 \\ &\approx 2.3660 \;\text{bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ **Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{2.3660}{2.40} \times 100\,\% \approx 98.58\,\%$$ #### Model Solution to (b): **Kraft Check:** $$ \sum_{i=1}^{6} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-2}+2^{-2}+2^{-2}+2^{-3}+2^{-4}+2^{-4} = 0.25+0.25+0.25+0.125+0.0625+0.0625 = 1.0 \le 1 $$ Equality holds → **complete** prefix tree ✔️. **Redundancy:** $$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9858 = 0.0142 \approx 1.42\,\%$$ **Length Uniqueness Argument:** Huffman coding's expected length is determined **solely by the sequence of merges**, which is uniquely determined by the probabilities (up to tie-breaking order). Since tie-breaking only swaps codewords *within* a length class — never changes the multiset of lengths — the resulting length distribution is unique. Hence, $\bar{L}$ and $\sigma^2$ are **invariant** across all valid Huffman trees for the same source. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** assign codewords before completing the *full* tree — partial assignment is the #1 mistake. > 2. **Do not** forget the final $\eta$ calculation — KTU awards **2 full marks** for efficiency; many students skip it. > 3. **Do not** confuse $H(S)$ with $\bar{L}$ in the final answer — keep them as *separate* numerically computed quantities. > 4. **Tie-breaking at the *last* merge matters for *which* symbol gets the '0' vs '1'**, but not for the length multiset. --- **Q3(B) — Alternative Choice.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Define the **coding efficiency** and **redundancy** of a source code. A source has 4 symbols with probabilities $\{0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1\}$. Construct the Huffman code, find $\bar{L}$ and $\eta$. (7 marks) **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **source encoding and decoding** process using Huffman coding. What happens to efficiency if the source becomes memoryless and symbols are coded in **blocks of two**? (7 marks) #### Model Solution Outline to (a): Sort $\{0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1\}$. Merge 0.2+0.1 = 0.3 → list $\{0.4, 0.3^*, 0.3^*\}$. Merge 0.3+0.3 = 0.6 → list $\{0.6, 0.4\}$. Merge → root. | Symbol | $p_i$ | Code | $l_i$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | A | 0.4 | 1 | 1 | | B | 0.3 | 00 | 2 | | C | 0.2 | 010 | 3 | | D | 0.1 | 011 | 3 | $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{L} &= (0.4)(1)+(0.3)(2)+(0.2)(3)+(0.1)(3) \\ &= 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.3 = 1.90 \end{aligned} $$ $$ H(S) = -[0.4\log_2 0.4 + 0.3\log_2 0.3 + 0.2\log_2 0.2 + 0.1\log_2 0.1] \approx 1.8464 $$ $$ \eta = \frac{1.8464}{1.90} \times 100\,\% \approx 97.18\,\% $$ #### Model Solution Outline to (b): **Block diagram:** [Refer to Section 4.3 above.] **Block-of-two extension:** - Pairwise joint probabilities $p_{ij} = p_i p_j$ form a 16-symbol source. - Huffman-code the joint source → new $\bar{L}_2$ bits per *pair*. - Per-symbol length = $\bar{L}_2/2$. - **As block size $n \to \infty$**, this per-symbol length $\to H(S)$ by Shannon's theorem. - Hence, **efficiency increases** with block size, approaching 100 %. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often state "efficiency becomes 100 %" — this is only true *asymptotically*. For finite blocks, it improves but does not reach unity. **Lose 1 mark** if you omit the asymptotic qualifier. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Definition:** Huffman code = optimal prefix-free variable-length code minimizing $\bar{L}$. - [x] **Algorithm:** Repeatedly merge the two *lowest* probabilities until one node (root) remains; assign 0/1 to left/right branches. - [x] **Average Length:** $\bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i$ — *always* a numerical summation, not a formula memorization. - [x] **Entropy:** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the **lower bound** for $\bar{L}$. - [x] **Efficiency:** $\eta = H(S)/\bar{L}$; **Redundancy:** $R = 1 - \eta$. - [x] **Kraft Inequality:** $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ — verify at the end of every problem. - [x] **Optimality Bound:** $H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$. - [x] **Length Uniqueness:** The multiset of code lengths is unique (tie-breaking only permutes within lengths). - [x] **Block Extension:** Block size $n$ → efficiency improves, $\to 100\%$ as $n \to \infty$. - [x] **Kraft Equality (1.0):** Indicates a *complete* binary tree; less than 1.0 means some branches are unused. - [x] **Application Domains:** ZIP, JPEG, MP3, PNG, fax, BLE, firmware bootloaders. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Mantra:** For any Huffman problem in the exam — **(1)** Draw the tree, **(2)** Read codewords, **(3)** Compute $\bar{L}$, **(4)** Compute $H(S)$, **(5)** Compute $\eta$, **(6)** Verify Kraft. **Skipping any one step costs 1–2 marks.** Always present values to **4 decimal places** for entropy, and 2 decimals for $\eta$ in %. ---

topics.title: Shannon's Source Coding Theorem (Achievability and Converse) and Operational Meaning of Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shannon's Source Coding Theorem (Achievability and Converse) and Operational Meaning of Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Shannon's Source Coding Theorem

Shannon's Source Coding Theorem (SSC Theorem), also known as the **Noiseless Coding Theorem**, establishes the fundamental limit of lossless data compression. For a Discrete Memoryless Source (DMS) $X$ with finite alphabet $\mathcal{A}$ and entropy $H(X)$ bits per symbol, the theorem is formally stated as:

> **Shannon's Source Coding Theorem (SSC Theorem):** Let $\{X_i\}$ be i.i.d. random variables drawn from a distribution with entropy $H(X)$. For any rate $R \geq H(X)$, there exists a uniquely decodable source code of rate $R$ such that the probability of decoding error $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Conversely, if $R < H(X)$, then for every block code of rate $R$, the probability of error is bounded away from zero (i.e., $P_e^{(n)} \geq \delta > 0$ for some $\delta$).

The theorem comprises two fundamental halves:

- **Achievability (Direct Part):** Proves the existence of compression schemes that approach rate $H(X)$ with vanishing error.
- **Converse (Reverse Part):** Proves that no compression scheme can beat rate $H(X)$ while maintaining arbitrarily low error.

### 1.2 Operational Meaning of Entropy

The *operational meaning* of entropy is its interpretation as a **physical, measurable quantity** rather than a purely abstract mathematical construct. Specifically:

> **Operational Meaning:** $H(X)$ is the minimum number of bits per symbol required to losslessly encode a DMS in the asymptotic limit of arbitrarily large block lengths with vanishing probability of error.

This is profound because it transforms entropy from a measure of *uncertainty* into a measure of *compressibility* — the two are equivalent.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a vast **library of weather reports** where 75% of days are sunny, 15% rainy, and 10% snowy in Kerala. Instead of writing the words "SUNNY," "RAINY," "SNOWY" with fixed-length codes (which wastes bits), a clever meteorologist assigns:

- `0` → SUNNY (1 bit, used most often)
- `10` → RAINY (2 bits)
- `11` → SNOWY (2 bits)

The **average code length** drops dramatically, approaching the entropy limit $H(X) \approx 1.16$ bits per day, instead of $\log_2 3 \approx 1.585$ bits if all were coded equally. The "magic" of approaching $H(X)$ comes from grouping long sequences of days and exploiting statistical regularities — this is the heart of the SSC Theorem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The SSC Theorem is a cornerstone of Module 1. Students must master the Achievability proof using **typical sets (AEP)** and the Converse proof using the **Kraft Inequality + Information Theoretic Inequality**. Expect a 14-mark Part B question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Asymptotic Equipartition Property (AEP) — The Backbone of Achievability

Before proving achievability, we must understand **AEP**, which is the *Weak Law of Large Numbers applied to information theory*:

For a sequence $X^n = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ drawn i.i.d. $\sim p(x)$, the **sample entropy** is defined as:

$$
\hat{H}(X^n) \triangleq -\frac{1}{n} \log p(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log p(X_i)
$$

The AEP states that as $n \to \infty$, the sample entropy converges (in probability) to the true entropy $H(X)$:

$$
\hat{H}(X^n) \xrightarrow{P} H(X)
$$

This convergence allows us to define the **typical set** $A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$:

$$
A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \triangleq \left\{ x^n \in \mathcal{A}^n : \left\vert -\frac{1}{n} \log p(x^n) - H(X) \right\vert \leq \varepsilon \right\}
$$

### 2.2 The Two-Phase Achievability Argument

**Phase 1 — Probabilistic Mass Concentration:**
By AEP and the Weak Law of Large Numbers:

$$
P\left(X^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}\right) \to 1 \quad \text{as } n \to \infty
$$

This means **almost all probability mass** is concentrated on the typical set.

**Phase 2 — Cardinality Bound:**
The typical set has size bounded by:

$$
(1-\varepsilon) \cdot 2^{n(H(X)-\varepsilon)} \leq \left\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \right\vert \leq 2^{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}
$$

Therefore, the typical set is **exponentially large but logarithmically small** compared to the full space $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^n$. We need only $\lceil n(H(X) + \varepsilon) \rceil$ bits to enumerate all typical sequences.

### 2.3 Encoding Strategy (Achievability Code)

1. **Allocate** $\lceil n(H(X) + \varepsilon) \rceil + 1$ bits.
2. **Use 1 bit** as a flag: `0` = typical sequence, `1` = atypical sequence.
3. **If typical**, assign a unique binary index (using the remaining bits).
4. **If atypical**, transmit a fixed string (e.g., all zeros) — decoder knows to default.

**Result:** As $n \to \infty$, the probability that any sequence is atypical vanishes, so the **average rate $\to H(X)$** and **error probability $\to 0$**.

### 2.4 The Converse Argument (Sketch)

The converse uses three ingredients:

- **Kraft Inequality:** $\sum_{x \in \mathcal{A}} 2^{-l(x)} \leq 1$ for any prefix code.
- **Information Inequality:** $H(X^n) \leq \mathbb{E}[l(X^n)]$ with equality iff the code is optimal.
- **Additivity of Entropy:** $H(X^n) = nH(X)$ for an i.i.d. source.

If a code achieves rate $R < H(X) - \delta$ with vanishing error, then by the information inequality and Kraft:

$$
nR \geq H(X^n) - n\varepsilon = nH(X) - n\varepsilon
$$

For small $\varepsilon < \delta$, we get $R \geq H(X) - \varepsilon > H(X) - \delta$, a **contradiction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Converse Matters:** Without it, achievability alone would only show $H(X)$ is a *sufficient* condition. The converse elevates $H(X)$ to a *necessary* condition — making it the true fundamental limit.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Meaning / Application** |
|----------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Entropy (Shannon) | $H(X) = -\sum_{x} p(x) \log_2 p(x)$ | Average information per symbol (bits/symbol) |
| Joint Entropy (i.i.d. block) | $H(X^n) = nH(X)$ | Entropy of $n$-symbol block |
| Sample Entropy | $\hat{H}(X^n) = -\frac{1}{n} \log p(X^n)$ | Empirical entropy of a single sequence |
| Typical Set Definition | $A_\varepsilon^{(n)} = \{x^n : \vert \hat{H}(X^n) - H(X) \vert \leq \varepsilon\}$ | Sequences with typical statistical behavior |
| Typical Set Lower Bound | $\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \geq (1-\varepsilon) \cdot 2^{n(H(X)-\varepsilon)}$ | Minimum cardinality |
| Typical Set Upper Bound | $\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \leq 2^{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}$ | Maximum cardinality |
| Typical Set Probability | $P(A_\varepsilon^{(n)}) \geq 1 - \varepsilon$ | Asymptotically, almost-certain occurrence |
| Kraft Inequality | $\sum_{x} 2^{-l(x)} \leq 1$ | Necessary condition for prefix code |
| Expected Code Length Lower Bound | $\mathbb{E}[l(X)] \geq H(X)$ | Source Coding Theorem bound |
| Achievable Rate Condition | $R > H(X)$ | Sufficient for vanishing error |
| Converse Condition | $R < H(X)$ | Insufficient for vanishing error |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** SSC Theorem underpins every modern compression standard — **ZIP (DEFLATE), PNG (DEFLATE), JPEG (DCT + Huffman), MP3 (Huffman on MDCT coefficients), and 5G voice codecs (AMR-WB)**. It defines the theoretical efficiency ceiling against which practical codecs are benchmarked.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cardinality of the Typical Set (Upper Bound)

**Statement:** For any DMS $X$ with alphabet $\mathcal{A}$ and $\varepsilon > 0$:

$$
\left\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \right\vert \leq 2^{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}
$$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &\geq \sum_{x^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}} p(x^n) \\
  &\geq \sum_{x^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}} 2^{-n(H(X)+\varepsilon)} \quad \text{(by definition of typical set)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first inequality holds because probabilities sum to at most 1. The second follows from the typical set condition $-\frac{1}{n}\log p(x^n) \leq H(X)+\varepsilon$, which implies $p(x^n) \geq 2^{-n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}$.

Since every term in the sum is at least $2^{-n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}$, the cardinality $\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert$ times this minimum cannot exceed 1. Therefore:

$$
\left\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \right\vert \cdot 2^{-n(H(X)+\varepsilon)} \leq 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \left\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \right\vert \leq 2^{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)} \quad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Achievability Part of the SSC Theorem

**Theorem (Achievability):** For any DMS with entropy $H(X)$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a block code of length $n$ with rate $R = H(X) + \varepsilon + \frac{1}{n}$ and probability of error $P_e^{(n)} \leq \varepsilon$.

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Define the code:**
Let $l = \lceil \log_2 \vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \rceil$ bits be used to encode the typical set. From the upper bound:

$$
l \leq \lceil n(H(X) + \varepsilon) \rceil
$$

Add one flag bit, so the total bit budget is $n(H(X) + \varepsilon) + 1$.

**Step 2 — Compute the rate:**
The rate $R_n$ (bits per source symbol) is:

$$
R_n = \frac{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)+1}{n} = H(X) + \varepsilon + \frac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the encoder:**
- If $X^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$, assign a unique binary index from $\{0,1\}^l$ and prepend flag bit `0`.
- Otherwise, transmit the flag bit `1` followed by a fixed dummy sequence.

**Step 4 — Define the decoder:**
- If flag bit is `0`, look up the unique index in the typical set table and recover $X^n$.
- If flag bit is `1`, declare an error (or output a default).

**Step 5 — Bound the error probability:**
The error event is precisely the event that $X^n \notin A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$. By the AEP (Weak Law of Large Numbers):

$$
P\left( \left\vert \hat{H}(X^n) - H(X) \right\vert > \varepsilon \right) \leq \varepsilon \quad \text{for } n \text{ large enough}
$$

Thus $P_e^{(n)} = P(X^n \notin A_\varepsilon^{(n)}) \leq \varepsilon$.

**Step 6 — Take the limit:**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} R_n = H(X) + \varepsilon, \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} P_e^{(n)} \leq \varepsilon
$$

Since $\varepsilon$ is arbitrary, we can make both $R \to H(X)$ and $P_e \to 0$ simultaneously. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation: Converse Part of the SSC Theorem

**Theorem (Converse):** If a sequence of block codes $\{C_n\}$ has rates $R_n < H(X) - \delta$ for some $\delta > 0$ and all $n$ large, then $P_e^{(n)} \geq \delta' > 0$ for some constant $\delta'$ depending only on $\delta$.

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Express expected length:**
For any uniquely decodable code $C_n$ with codeword lengths $l(x^n)$:

$$
nR_n = \mathbb{E}[l(X^n)] \geq H(X^n) = nH(X)
$$

The inequality $\mathbb{E}[l(X^n)] \geq H(X^n)$ is the fundamental lower bound from the **Noiseless Coding Theorem** (a direct consequence of the concavity of $\log$ and the Kraft inequality).

**Step 2 — Reinterpret via Fano's Inequality:**
Let $P_e^{(n)} = P(\hat{X}^n \neq X^n)$. By **Fano's Inequality**:

$$
H(X^n \vert \hat{X}^n) \leq h(P_e^{(n)}) + P_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A}^n \vert = h(P_e^{(n)}) + n P_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
$$

where $h(\cdot)$ is the binary entropy function.

**Step 3 — Chain the inequalities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
nH(X) = H(X^n) &= I(X^n; \hat{X}^n) + H(X^n \vert \hat{X}^n) \\
                &\leq H(\hat{X}^n) + h(P_e^{(n)}) + nP_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bound $H(\hat{X}^n)$:**
Since $\hat{X}^n$ is encoded in $nR_n$ bits:

$$
H(\hat{X}^n) \leq nR_n
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and isolate $P_e^{(n)}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
nH(X) &\leq nR_n + h(P_e^{(n)}) + nP_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert \\
H(X) - R_n &\leq \frac{h(P_e^{(n)})}{n} + P_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude:**
If $R_n \leq H(X) - \delta$ for some $\delta > 0$, then:

$$
\delta \leq \frac{h(P_e^{(n)})}{n} + P_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
$$

For large $n$, the first term $\to 0$, forcing $P_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert \geq \delta/2$, hence:

$$
P_e^{(n)} \geq \frac{\delta}{2 \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert} > 0 \quad \blacksquare
$$

This shows that no scheme can compress below $H(X)$ without incurring a persistent error floor.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: AEP vs. Converse Roles

| **Aspect** | **Achievability** | **Converse** |
|------------|------------------|--------------|
| Goal | Construct a code with $R \to H(X)$ and $P_e \to 0$ | Show $R < H(X)$ implies $P_e$ cannot vanish |
| Main Tool | AEP + Typical Set | Fano's Inequality + Kraft Inequality |
| Output | Existence proof | Impossibility proof |
| Rate Required | $R \geq H(X)$ | $R \geq H(X)$ (necessary) |
| Engineering Analogy | Showing ZIP achieves near-optimal compression | Why no compressor beats ZIP by more than vanishing margin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Achievability Encoding Pipeline

The following diagram maps the data flow and decision logic inside the **achievability proof** — how an $n$-symbol source block is encoded into bits and decoded back.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source X with entropy HX] --> B[Sample n iid symbols X1 to Xn]
    B --> C{Compute sample entropy -1 by n times log p of Xn}
    C --> D{Is sample entropy within eps of HX}
    D -- YES YES --> E[Typical sequence: xn in A eps superscript n]
    D -- NO NO --> F[Atypical sequence]
    E --> G[Assign unique binary index of length ceil n HX plus eps]
    G --> H[Prepend flag bit 0]
    F --> I[Transmit flag bit 1 plus fixed dummy]
    H --> J[Channel: transmit n bits]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Receiver checks flag bit}
    K -- Flag is 0 --> L[Look up index in typical set table]
    K -- Flag is 1 --> M[Declare error or default output]
    L --> N[Decode xn hat as estimate of xn]
    M --> O[Output dummy xn hat]
    N --> P[Decision: error if xn hat not equal xn]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Probability of error tends to 0 as n tends to infinity]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Architecture: The Operational Meaning of Entropy

This diagram shows how entropy functions as the **compression-rate floor** — a boundary that practical codes asymptotically approach but never undercut.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THEORETICAL_LIMIT [Theoretical Region]
        A[Rate R less than HX: IMPOSSIBLE to achieve vanishing error]
    end
    subgraph BOUNDARY [Fundamental Limit]
        B[Rate R equals HX: Achievable in the limit as n to infinity]
    end
    subgraph PRACTICAL_REGION [Practical Achievable Region]
        C[Rate R greater than HX: Achievable with small positive error eps]
        D[Rate R much greater than HX: Achievable with essentially zero error]
    end
    A -->|Converse Theorem| B
    B -->|Achievability Theorem| C
    C -->|More bits spent| D
    B -.->|Optimal Compression| E[ZIP, Huffman, Arithmetic, BWT]
    C -.->|Suboptimal Codes| F[Fixed length code, simple schemes]
    D -.->|Trivial but wasteful| G[Trivial encoding with large redundancy]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Entropy as Compression Benchmark

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discrete Memoryless Source X] --> B[Empirical Sequence X1 X2 ... Xn]
    B --> C[Encoder: Maps to binary codeword]
    C --> D[Binary Stream of length L bits]
    D --> E[Decoder: Recovers X hat]
    E --> F{Reconstruction Quality Check}
    F -- X hat equals X --> G[Successful Lossless Compression]
    F -- X hat not equal X --> H[Error Event]
    B --> I[Compute Average Length L divided by n]
    I --> J{Compare with HX}
    J -- L over n approaches HX --> K[Optimal: Achieves Fundamental Limit]
    J -- L over n greater than HX --> L[Suboptimal: Wastes Bits]
    J -- L over n less than HX --> M[Impossible: Error Cannot Vanish]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagrams:** In the KTU board examination, drawing clear block diagrams like the above earns **easy 1-2 marks** under "explain with neat sketch" type questions. Always label arrows with quantities (rate $R$, error $P_e$, etc.) to demonstrate conceptual command.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Shannon's Source Coding Theorem. What is its operational significance?**

**Model Answer:**

Shannon's Source Coding Theorem states that for a discrete memoryless source with entropy $H(X)$ bits per symbol:

- **Achievability:** For any $R > H(X)$, there exists a block code of length $n$ with rate $\leq R$ and $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Converse:** If $R < H(X)$, then for every sequence of block codes, $P_e^{(n)} \geq \delta > 0$ for some $\delta > 0$.

**Operational Significance:** Entropy $H(X)$ represents the **minimum achievable average number of bits per source symbol** required for lossless compression in the asymptotic limit. It transforms entropy from an abstract measure of uncertainty into a concrete *physical limit of compression*. This principle underlies every modern lossless codec — ZIP, PNG, FLAC, and arithmetic coding schemes all approach this fundamental bound.

---

**Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define typical set and Asymptotic Equipartition Property (AEP).**

**Model Answer:**

**Asymptotic Equipartition Property (AEP):** For a sequence of i.i.d. random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ drawn from distribution $p(x)$ with entropy $H(X)$:

$$
-\frac{1}{n} \log p(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) \xrightarrow{P} H(X)
$$

That is, the negative log-probability per symbol converges (in probability) to the true entropy.

**Typical Set:** For a given $\varepsilon > 0$, the typical set $A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$ is defined as:

$$
A_\varepsilon^{(n)} = \left\{ x^n \in \mathcal{A}^n : \left\vert -\frac{1}{n} \log p(x^n) - H(X) \right\vert \leq \varepsilon \right\}
$$

It contains all sequences whose empirical entropy lies within $\varepsilon$ of $H(X)$. The AEP ensures that $P(A_\varepsilon^{(n)}) \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$, while the size of the typical set is approximately $2^{nH(X)}$ — exponentially smaller than the total $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^n$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Module Question (Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and prove the **Achievability part** of Shannon's Source Coding Theorem using the Asymptotic Equipartition Property. (7 Marks)

**(b)** State and prove the **Converse part** of Shannon's Source Coding Theorem using Fano's Inequality. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Achievability Proof:**

**Statement:** Let $X$ be a DMS with entropy $H(X)$. For any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a block code of length $n$ with rate $R_n \leq H(X) + \varepsilon$ and probability of error $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** By AEP, $P(A_\varepsilon^{(n)}) \geq 1 - \varepsilon$ for sufficiently large $n$, where $A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$ is the typical set.

**Step 2:** From the upper bound on typical set size, $\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \leq 2^{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}$.

**Step 3:** Allocate $\lceil n(H(X) + \varepsilon) \rceil + 1$ bits:
- 1 bit flag distinguishes typical from atypical.
- Remaining bits assign a unique index to each typical sequence.

**Step 4:** The rate is:

$$
R_n = \frac{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)+1}{n} = H(X) + \varepsilon + \frac{1}{n}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, $R_n \to H(X) + \varepsilon$.

**Step 5:** The error occurs only when $X^n \notin A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$:

$$
P_e^{(n)} = P(X^n \notin A_\varepsilon^{(n)}) \leq \varepsilon
$$

**Step 6:** Since $\varepsilon$ is arbitrary, by choosing $\varepsilon \to 0$ and $n \to \infty$, both rate $\to H(X)$ and error $\to 0$ are achieved simultaneously. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Converse Proof:**

**Statement:** If $R_n < H(X) - \delta$ for some $\delta > 0$ and all $n$ sufficiently large, then $P_e^{(n)} \geq \delta' > 0$ for some $\delta'$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Let $\hat{X}^n$ denote the decoded sequence. By Fano's Inequality:

$$
H(X^n \vert \hat{X}^n) \leq h(P_e^{(n)}) + nP_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
$$

**Step 2:** Expand mutual information:

$$
H(X^n) = I(X^n; \hat{X}^n) + H(X^n \vert \hat{X}^n) \leq H(\hat{X}^n) + h(P_e^{(n)}) + nP_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
$$

**Step 3:** Since $\hat{X}^n$ takes values in a set of size at most $2^{nR_n}$:

$$
H(\hat{X}^n) \leq nR_n
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $H(X^n) = nH(X)$ (i.i.d. property):

$$
nH(X) \leq nR_n + h(P_e^{(n)}) + nP_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:

$$
H(X) - R_n \leq \frac{h(P_e^{(n)})}{n} + P_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert
$$

**Step 6:** If $R_n \leq H(X) - \delta$, then $\delta \leq \frac{h(P_e^{(n)})}{n} + P_e^{(n)} \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$. For $n \to \infty$, the first term vanishes, so:

$$
P_e^{(n)} \geq \frac{\delta}{2 \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert} > 0 \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the typical set $A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$. Derive the upper and lower bounds on its cardinality. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **operational meaning of entropy** with a suitable example. Why is it called the "fundamental limit of lossless compression"? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cardinality Bounds on $A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$:**

**Upper Bound:**

By definition, every $x^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}$ satisfies $-\frac{1}{n}\log p(x^n) \leq H(X) + \varepsilon$, giving $p(x^n) \geq 2^{-n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}$. Summing over the typical set:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \geq \sum_{x^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}} p(x^n) \geq \sum_{x^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}} 2^{-n(H(X)+\varepsilon)} = \vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \cdot 2^{-n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \leq 2^{n(H(X)+\varepsilon)}$. $\blacksquare$

**Lower Bound:**

By AEP, $P(A_\varepsilon^{(n)}) \geq 1 - \varepsilon$. Combining with the lower-bound probability per sequence $p(x^n) \leq 2^{-n(H(X)-\varepsilon)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1-\varepsilon \leq P(A_\varepsilon^{(n)}) = \sum_{x^n \in A_\varepsilon^{(n)}} p(x^n) \leq \vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \cdot 2^{-n(H(X)-\varepsilon)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \geq (1-\varepsilon) \cdot 2^{n(H(X)-\varepsilon)}$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Operational Meaning of Entropy:**

Entropy $H(X)$ is the **minimum average number of bits per source symbol** needed to encode a DMS losslessly, in the asymptotic limit of vanishing error probability. This is its **operational meaning** — it transforms a mathematical construct into an engineering benchmark.

**Example:** Consider a binary source with $P(X=0) = 0.99$, $P(X=1) = 0.01$. Its entropy is:

$$
H(X) = -0.99 \log_2 0.99 - 0.01 \log_2 0.01 \approx 0.0808 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

A naïve fixed-length code would use 1 bit per symbol — wasting $\sim 92\%$ of the bits. By using Shannon-Fano or Huffman coding on long blocks, we approach $H(X) \approx 0.0808$ bits/symbol, achieving near-perfect compression.

**Why "Fundamental Limit":**

- It is a **tight bound** — Achievability proves codes can approach it; Converse proves no code can beat it.
- It is **universal** — applies to *any* DMS regardless of distribution shape.
- It is **achievable in principle** — practical codecs (Lempel-Ziv, BWT, arithmetic) all converge to it for large blocks.

Thus, $H(X)$ is the *fundamental limit* of lossless compression: the compression rate cannot drop below $H(X)$ without incurring irreducible error.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the flag bit:** Students often state the rate as $H(X) + \varepsilon$ and miss the $+\frac{1}{n}$ overhead. Examiners deduct 1 mark. Always include the bookkeeping flag.
> 2. **Confusing AEP with Chebyshev:** AEP is a *Weak Law of Large Numbers* statement on $-\frac{1}{n}\log p(X^n)$, NOT on the raw $X_i$. Writing "$\bar{X} \to \mu$" instead of the log-likelihood loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Converse Proof Skips Fano:** Using only the Kraft inequality is incomplete. The full converse requires **Fano's Inequality** to link decoding error to mutual information. Examiners expect this explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the "+1" in $2^{nH+\varepsilon}$ upper bound:** The inequality $\vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \leq 2^{n(H+\varepsilon)}$ is sharp only for integer logarithms; indicate the ceiling for full marks.
> 5. **Operational Meaning ≠ Definition:** Many students *define* entropy but fail to articulate its *physical significance*. KTU examiners award marks for the phrase "fundamental limit of lossless compression" — use it explicitly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - $\blacksquare$ SSC Theorem has **two parts**: Achievability (using AEP/Typical Set) and Converse (using Fano's Inequality).
> - $\blacksquare$ **Achievability rate:** $R_n = H(X) + \varepsilon + \frac{1}{n}$, **error:** $P_e^{(n)} \leq \varepsilon$.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Converse implication:** $R < H(X) - \delta \Rightarrow P_e^{(n)} \geq \delta / (2 \log \vert \mathcal{A} \vert) > 0$.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Typical Set Cardinality:** $(1-\varepsilon) \cdot 2^{n(H-\varepsilon)} \leq \vert A_\varepsilon^{(n)} \vert \leq 2^{n(H+\varepsilon)}$.
> - $\blacksquare$ **AEP:** $-\frac{1}{n} \log p(X^n) \xrightarrow{P} H(X)$.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Kraft Inequality:** $\sum 2^{-l(x)} \leq 1$ — required for prefix codes.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Fano's Inequality:** $H(X^n \vert \hat{X}^n) \leq h(P_e) + P_e \log \vert \mathcal{A}^n \vert$ — key to the converse.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Operational Meaning:** $H(X)$ = minimum bits/symbol for **lossless compression** of a DMS as $n \to \infty$.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Engineering Relevance:** Direct basis of ZIP, PNG, MP3, JPEG, FLAC codecs.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Mnemonic:** **"AEP → Achievability, Fano → Converse, Kraft → Both"** — keeps proofs straight under exam pressure.
> - $\blacksquare$ **Critical Phrase for KTU Answers:** Always conclude with "*$H(X)$ is the fundamental limit of lossless source coding*."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Memoryless Channels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Memoryless Channels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete Memoryless Channel (DMC):** A Discrete Memoryless Channel is a communication channel defined by a finite input alphabet $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, a finite output alphabet $\mathcal{Y} = \{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_m\}$, and a set of conditional transition probabilities $P(y_j \mid x_i)$ for all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ and $j \in \{1, \dots, m\}$, such that the output at any time instant depends **only** on the input at that same time instant and **not** on any previous or future inputs or outputs.

Mathematically, for any sequence of inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$ and outputs $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_N$:

$$P(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_N \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} P(y_k \mid x_k)$$

The above factorization is the **memoryless property** and is the cornerstone of DMC analysis.

### 1.2 Key Properties of a DMC

| Property | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Discrete** | Both input alphabet $\mathcal{X}$ and output alphabet $\mathcal{Y}$ are finite (or countably finite) sets. |
| **Memoryless** | Channel has no memory; current output depends solely on current input. |
| **Time-Invariant** | Transition probabilities $P(y \mid x)$ do not change with time. |
| **Causal / Non-anticipative** | Output at time $k$ cannot be influenced by inputs at time $k+1, k+2, \dots$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Tainted Letter Box":**
> Imagine you are a student slipping handwritten letters into a dusty old letterbox. Each letter (input symbol $x_i$) reaches your friend on the other side, but the dust and the wind might smudge or rip the paper. The smudging depends *only* on the single letter you slipped in at that moment — it doesn't matter what you sent yesterday or what you will send tomorrow. The friend receives a *possibly-corrupted* version (output symbol $y_j$). The "rules" governing how letters get smudged are fixed (time-invariant), and a torn letter is never repaired by remembering past letters (memoryless). This letterbox is your **Discrete Memoryless Channel**.

### 1.4 Channel Transition Probability Matrix

The collection of all transition probabilities forms the **channel matrix** (also called the **probability transition matrix** or **stochastic matrix**):

$$\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} P(y_1 \mid x_1) & P(y_2 \mid x_1) & \cdots & P(y_m \mid x_1) \\ P(y_1 \mid x_2) & P(y_2 \mid x_2) & \cdots & P(y_m \mid x_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ P(y_1 \mid x_n) & P(y_2 \mid x_n) & \cdots & P(y_m \mid x_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

- Each **row** corresponds to a fixed input symbol.
- Each **column** corresponds to a fixed output symbol.
- The **row sums** must equal 1: $\sum_{j=1}^{m} P(y_j \mid x_i) = 1$ for all $i$.
- All entries are non-negative: $P(y_j \mid x_i) \geq 0$.
- The channel is called **symmetric** if every row of $\mathbf{Q}$ is a permutation of every other row and every column is a permutation of every other column.
- The channel is called **weakly symmetric** if every row of $\mathbf{Q}$ is a permutation of every other row and the column sums are all equal.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC)** and **Binary Erasure Channel (BEC)** are the two canonical DMC models studied in KTU 2024 syllabus. Their derivations are given in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics of a DMC

A DMC operates as a probabilistic mapping $\mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ governed entirely by the transition probabilities. The mechanism unfolds as follows:

1. **Source generates** an input symbol $x_i \in \mathcal{X}$ with prior probability $P(x_i)$.
2. **Channel corrupts** $x_i$ stochastically and emits $y_j \in \mathcal{Y}$.
3. **Transition probability** $P(y_j \mid x_i)$ is governed by physical channel noise (thermal, interference, etc.).
4. **Output distribution** at the receiver is obtained by the law of total probability:

$$P(y_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(y_j \mid x_i) \, P(x_i)$$

5. **Joint probability** of input-output pair:

$$P(x_i, y_j) = P(y_j \mid x_i) \, P(x_i)$$

6. **Posterior / A-posteriori probability** (via Bayes' rule):

$$P(x_i \mid y_j) = \frac{P(y_j \mid x_i) \, P(x_i)}{P(y_j)} = \frac{P(y_j \mid x_i) \, P(x_i)}{\sum_{k} P(y_j \mid x_k) P(x_k)}$$

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Memoryless Property

The memoryless condition is not a limitation — it is a **mathematical idealization** that allows tractable analysis. Real channels (wireless, optical, storage) have memory due to fading, burst errors, or ISI. However, when:
- Channel coding is interleaved, or
- The symbol interval is long compared to the channel's coherence time,

the channel can be effectively modeled as memoryless, and Shannon's theorems apply.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists all KTU-exam-relevant formulas for Discrete Memoryless Channels. Memorize the equations and the units/conditions precisely.

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Conditions / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Memoryless Property | $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} P(y_k \mid x_k)$ | $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X}^N \times \mathcal{Y}^N$ |
| 2 | Row-Sum Constraint of $\mathbf{Q}$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{m} P(y_j \mid x_i) = 1$ | For every $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ |
| 3 | Output Marginal | $P(y_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(y_j \mid x_i) P(x_i)$ | Law of Total Probability |
| 4 | Joint Probability | $P(x_i, y_j) = P(y_j \mid x_i) P(x_i)$ | Product rule of probability |
| 5 | Bayes' Rule (Decoding) | $P(x_i \mid y_j) = \frac{P(y_j \mid x_i) P(x_i)}{P(y_j)}$ | Posterior probability |
| 6 | Mutual Information (per symbol) | $I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$ | Measured in **bits/symbol** |
| 7 | Conditional Entropy (Noise) | $H(Y \mid X) = -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i, y_j) \log_2 P(y_j \mid x_i)$ | **bits/symbol** |
| 8 | Channel Capacity (preliminary) | $C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y)$ | **bits/channel use** |
| 9 | BSC Transition Prob. | $P(y \mid x)$: $P(\text{correct}) = 1-p$, $P(\text{error}) = p$ | $0 \leq p \leq 0.5$ |
| 10 | BEC Erasure Probability | $P(\text{erased} \mid x) = \epsilon$, $P(\text{correct} \mid x) = 1 - \epsilon$ | $0 \leq \epsilon \leq 1$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study DMCs in engineering?**
> - **Satellite Communication:** DMCs model bit-flips due to thermal noise.
> - **Data Storage (HDD/SSD):** Bit-errors in memory cells are modeled as BSC.
> - **Packet Networks:** BEC models packet drops in routers / wireless links.
> - **Cellular 5G/6G:** Coding schemes (LDPC, Polar) are designed using DMC capacity bounds.
> - **DNA Storage:** Sequencing noise in DNA-based data storage is a DMC.

### 2.5 Decoding Strategies in DMCs

For a DMC, the **Maximum A-Posteriori (MAP)** decoder is:

$$\hat{x}_{MAP}(y_j) = \arg\max_{x_i} P(x_i \mid y_j) = \arg\max_{x_i} P(y_j \mid x_i) P(x_i)$$

The **Maximum Likelihood (ML)** decoder (used when inputs are equiprobable) is:

$$\hat{x}_{ML}(y_j) = \arg\max_{x_i} P(y_j \mid x_i)$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC):** $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}$, $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1\}$, and a bit-flip occurs with probability $p$, with the bit being received correctly with probability $1-p$.

**Step 1 — Set up the alphabet.**

$$\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}, \quad \mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1\}$$

**Step 2 — Define the transition probabilities.**

If input is $x = 0$, then output $y = 0$ with probability $1-p$ and $y = 1$ with probability $p$.

$$P(y=0 \mid x=0) = 1 - p, \quad P(y=1 \mid x=0) = p$$

If input is $x = 1$, then output $y = 1$ with probability $1-p$ and $y = 0$ with probability $p$.

$$P(y=1 \mid x=1) = 1 - p, \quad P(y=0 \mid x=1) = p$$

**Step 3 — Write the channel matrix.**

$$\mathbf{Q}_{BSC} = \begin{bmatrix} 1-p & p \\ p & 1-p \end{bmatrix}$$

The first row corresponds to input $x=0$, the second row to input $x=1$. The first column to output $y=0$, the second to output $y=1$.

**Step 4 — Verify row-sum constraint.**

$$(1-p) + p = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad p + (1-p) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Compute output marginal assuming equiprobable input** $P(x=0) = P(x=1) = 0.5$.

$$P(y=0) = P(y=0 \mid x=0) P(x=0) + P(y=0 \mid x=1) P(x=1)$$

$$P(y=0) = (1-p)(0.5) + p(0.5) = 0.5(1-p+p) = 0.5$$

$$P(y=1) = p(0.5) + (1-p)(0.5) = 0.5$$

**Step 6 — Compute the conditional entropy $H(Y \mid X)$ (this is the noise entropy of BSC).**

$$H(Y \mid X) = -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i, y_j) \log_2 P(y_j \mid x_i)$$

Using $P(x_i, y_j) = P(y_j \mid x_i) P(x_i)$ with $P(x_i) = 0.5$:

$$H(Y \mid X) = -2 \left[ \tfrac{1}{2}(1-p) \log_2(1-p) + \tfrac{1}{2} p \log_2 p \right]$$

$$H(Y \mid X) = -(1-p) \log_2(1-p) - p \log_2 p$$

$$H(Y \mid X) = H(p) = H_2(p) \quad \text{(Binary Entropy Function)}$$

$$H(Y \mid X) = H_2(p) \;\; \textbf{bits per channel use}$$

**Step 7 — Compute $H(Y)$ assuming equiprobable input.**

Since the BSC is symmetric and input is equiprobable, output is also equiprobable: $P(y=0) = P(y=1) = 0.5$.

$$H(Y) = -2 \cdot 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1 \;\; \text{bit}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the mutual information $I(X;Y)$ for equiprobable input.**

$$I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X) = 1 - H_2(p) \;\; \text{bits/channel use}$$

**Step 9 — Channel capacity of BSC** (achieved with equiprobable input, by symmetry):

$$C_{BSC} = 1 - H_2(p) \;\; \text{bits/channel use}$$

**Sanity check:** When $p = 0$ (noiseless), $H_2(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C = 1$ bit. When $p = 0.5$ (pure noise), $H_2(0.5) = 1 \Rightarrow C = 0$ bits. Correct.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Binary Erasure Channel (BEC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Erasure Channel (BEC):** $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}$, $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1, e\}$, where $e$ is the erasure symbol. The bit is received correctly with probability $1-\epsilon$ and erased with probability $\epsilon$.

**Step 1 — Set up the alphabet.**

$$\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}, \quad \mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1, e\}$$

**Step 2 — Define the transition probabilities.**

$$P(y=0 \mid x=0) = 1 - \epsilon, \quad P(y=e \mid x=0) = \epsilon, \quad P(y=1 \mid x=0) = 0$$

$$P(y=1 \mid x=1) = 1 - \epsilon, \quad P(y=e \mid x=1) = \epsilon, \quad P(y=0 \mid x=1) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Write the channel matrix.**

$$\mathbf{Q}_{BEC} = \begin{bmatrix} 1-\epsilon & 0 & \epsilon \\ 0 & 1-\epsilon & \epsilon \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $H(Y \mid X)$.**

When $X = x$, the output is either $x$ (with prob $1-\epsilon$) or $e$ (with prob $\epsilon$). The output alphabet given input $x$ is binary $\{x, e\}$.

$$H(Y \mid X = x) = -(1-\epsilon) \log_2(1-\epsilon) - \epsilon \log_2 \epsilon = H_2(\epsilon)$$

This is independent of $x$, so:

$$H(Y \mid X) = H_2(\epsilon) \;\; \text{bits/channel use}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $H(Y)$ assuming equiprobable input.**

$$P(y=0) = (1-\epsilon)(0.5) = \tfrac{1-\epsilon}{2}, \quad P(y=1) = \tfrac{1-\epsilon}{2}, \quad P(y=e) = \epsilon$$

$$H(Y) = -2 \cdot \tfrac{1-\epsilon}{2} \log_2 \tfrac{1-\epsilon}{2} - \epsilon \log_2 \epsilon$$
