<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem. Distinguish between its achievability and converse parts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem Statement:** For a DMC with capacity $C = \max_{p(x)} I(X;Y)$, all rates $R < C$ are achievable (i.e., there exists a sequence of $(2^{nR}, n)$ codes with $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$). Conversely, if $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$, then necessarily $R \leq C$.

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Achievability | Converse |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Information-theoretic $\Rightarrow$ Operational | Operational $\Rightarrow$ Information-theoretic |
| Proves | $R < C \Rightarrow P_e \to 0$ possible | $P_e \to 0 \Rightarrow R \leq C$ must hold |
| Method | Random coding + joint typicality | Fano's inequality + data processing |
| Tools | AEP, union bound | Memoryless bound, chain rule |

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the operational meaning of channel capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The operational meaning of channel capacity $C$ is the **maximum rate** (in bits per channel use) at which reliable communication is possible over a noisy DMC, in the limit of long block codes. Specifically:

1. **Upper-bound on reliable rates:** $C$ is the supremum of all rates $R$ such that there exist $(2^{nR}, n)$ codes with $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.
2. **Speed limit analogy:** It represents the fundamental trade-off — communicating faster than $C$ guarantees non-vanishing error.
3. **Equivalence to algebraic form:** By Shannon's theorem, this operational $C$ equals the algebraic quantity $\max_{p(x)} I(X;Y)$.

In short, capacity is the **engineering speed limit of reliable information transfer** over a given channel.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State and prove the **converse** of Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem. Clearly explain the role of Fano's inequality in the proof. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **Binary Erasure Channel (BEC)** with erasure probability $\epsilon = 0.25$, determine the channel capacity $C$ and verify whether a rate $R = 0.6$ bits/channel use is achievable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a): Converse Proof (7 marks)

**Statement (1 mark):** If a sequence of $(2^{nR}, n)$ codes satisfies $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, then $R \leq C$.

**Proof (6 marks):**

Let $M$ be uniform over $\{1, 2, \ldots, 2^{nR}\}$ and $\hat{M}$ be the decoded message.

**Step 1: Fano's Inequality (2 marks)**
$$
H(M \mid \hat{M}) \leq h(P_e) + P_e \log(2^{nR}) \leq 1 + n R P_e^{(n)}
$$
Defining $\epsilon_n = \frac{1}{n} + R P_e^{(n)}$, note that $\epsilon_n \to 0$ since $P_e^{(n)} \to 0$.

**Step 2: Expand $H(M)$ (1 mark)**
$$
nR = H(M) = I(M; \hat{M}) + H(M \mid \hat{M}) \leq I(M; \hat{M}) + n\epsilon_n
$$

**Step 3: Data Processing Inequality (1 mark)**
By the Markov chain $M \to X^n \to Y^n \to \hat{M}$:
$$
I(M; \hat{M}) \leq I(X^n; Y^n)
$$

**Step 4: Memoryless Bound (1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I(X^n; Y^n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} I(X_i; Y_i) \quad \text{(memoryless)} \\
&\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} C = nC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Conclude (1 mark)**
$$
R \leq C + \epsilon_n \xrightarrow{n \to \infty} R \leq C \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget that Fano's inequality requires $\log$ base matching with rate units. Mixing $\log_2$ with $n$ in nats causes dimensional mismatch and immediate 1-mark deduction.

---

#### Solution to Part (b): BEC Capacity (7 marks)

**Step 1: Capacity Formula (2 marks)**
For BEC with erasure probability $\epsilon$:
$$
C_{BEC} = (1 - \epsilon) \log_2 2 + \epsilon \cdot 0 = 1 - \epsilon \text{ bits per channel use}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute (1 mark)**
$$
C = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ bits/channel use}
$$

**Step 3: Achievability Check (2 marks)**
Given $R = 0.6$ bits/channel use. Compare:
$$
R = 0.6 < C = 0.75 \checkmark
$$

Since $R < C$, by Shannon's channel coding theorem, there exists a sequence of $(2^{n \cdot 0.6}, n)$ block codes such that:
$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} P_e^{(n)} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Verification via Information Theoretic Bound (2 marks)**
The maximum mutual information is achieved when the input is uniform:
$$
I(X; Y) = 1 \cdot (1 - \epsilon) = 0.75 \text{ bits}
$$

Since $R = 0.6 < 0.75$, the achievability condition is satisfied. **Therefore $R = 0.6$ is achievable.**

---

### Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **operational meaning of channel capacity** with an illustrative example. Show that the operational definition coincides with the information-theoretic definition $C = \max_{p(x)} I(X;Y)$ via Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) has crossover probability $p = 0.05$. Using the asymptotic equipartition property (AEP), outline the proof of the **achievability part** of Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem and hence compute the capacity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a): Operational Meaning (7 marks)

**Operational Definition (3 marks):**
The operational meaning of $C$ is the supremum of all reliably-achievable rates:
$$
C = \sup \left\{ R : \exists \text{ sequence of } (2^{nR}, n) \text{ codes with } P_e^{(n)} \to 0 \right\}
$$

**Illustrative Example (2 marks):** Consider a BSC with $p = 0.1$. Here $C = 1 - H_2(0.1) \approx 0.531$. Operationally, this means:
- Sending information at $R = 0.4$ bits/use with long block codes (say $n = 1000$) yields error probability $< 10^{-10}$.
- Sending at $R = 0.6$ bits/use is *impossible* — error probability stays bounded away from 0.

**Equivalence via Theorem (2 marks):** Shannon's theorem proves:
- Achievability: $\forall R < \max I(X;Y)$, $\exists$ reliable codes.
- Converse: Reliable codes $\Rightarrow R \leq \max I(X;Y)$.

Thus $C_{\text{operational}} = C_{\text{information-theoretic}} = \max_{p(x)} I(X;Y)$.

---

#### Solution to Part (b): Achievability via AEP (7 marks)

**Step 1: Capacity Computation (1 mark)**
$$
C_{BSC} = 1 - H_2(0.05)
$$

Computing $H_2(0.05)$:
$$
-0.05 \log_2 0.05 = 0.05 \times 4.3219 = 0.2161
$$
$$
-0.95 \log_2 0.95 = 0.95 \times 0.0740 = 0.0703
$$

$$
H_2(0.05) = 0.2161 + 0.0703 = 0.2864 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
C = 1 - 0.2864 = 0.7136 \text{ bits/channel use}
$$

**Step 2: Achievability Proof Outline (5 marks)**

**(i) Random Codebook (1 mark):** Fix input distribution $p^*(x) = \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ (achieves $C$). Generate $M = 2^{nR}$ codewords i.i.d.

**(ii) Decoder (1 mark):** Use joint typical decoding: declare $\hat{m}$ if $(X^n(\hat{m}), Y^n)$ is jointly typical and unique.

**(iii) Error Probability Bound (2 marks):** Using AEP:
- $P(\text{atypical}) \leq \epsilon$ for large $n$.
- $P(\text{spurious match per } m') \leq 2^{-n[I(X;Y) - \epsilon']}$.

By union bound:
$$
P_e \leq \epsilon + (2^{nR} - 1) \cdot 2^{-n[I(X;Y) - \epsilon']} \leq \epsilon + 2^{-n[C - R - \epsilon']}
$$

**(iv) Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $R < C$, the exponent is positive and $P_e \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. By averaging, some codebook achieves this. **∎**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common 14-mark Question Errors:**
> 1. **Failing to state AEP** before using typical set sizes — lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the averaging argument** to convert random code expectation to existence — lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up achievability and converse tools:** Random coding is for achievability; Fano's inequality is for converse. Wrong attribution loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not taking the limit $n \to \infty$** explicitly in the conclusion — lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem**

- **Theorem Statement:** $C = \max_{p(x)} I(X;Y)$ is both the algebraic maximum and the operational rate limit for reliable DMC communication.
- **Achievability** says: $R < C \Rightarrow \exists$ codes with $P_e \to 0$. Uses **random coding** + **joint typical decoding** + **AEP**.
- **Converse** says: $P_e \to 0 \Rightarrow R \leq C$. Uses **Fano's inequality** + **data processing** + **memoryless bound**.
- **Random coding argument** is the source of *existence*, not *construction* — practical codes (LDPC, Turbo, Polar) approach capacity through different means.
- **Joint typical set** $A_{\epsilon}^{(n)}$ has size $\approx 2^{nH(X,Y)}$ and probability of *independent* pair falling in it $\approx 2^{-nI(X;Y)}$.
- **Fano's inequality:** $H(M \mid \hat{M}) \leq 1 + P_e \log M$ — quantifies residual uncertainty after decoding.
- **Exponential decay:** $P_e$ decays as $2^{-n(C-R)}$ for fixed $R < C$ — this is the *coding gain*.
- **BSC capacity:** $C = 1 - H_2(p)$ — symmetric, maximized at $p = 0$ or $p = 1$ (zero noise).
- **BEC capacity:** $C = 1 - \epsilon$ — erasures cost linearly, no equivocation.
- **Existence vs. Construction:** Shannon proved existence; modern coding theory finds explicit constructions.
- **Block length trade-off:** Longer $n$ gives lower $P_e$ but higher complexity and latency — practical systems operate at finite $n$.
- **Information units:** Capacity $C$ is in **bits/channel use** (base 2) unless otherwise specified.
- **Channel memory:** Theorem assumes DMC (memoryless); extensions exist for channels with memory (Gaussian, MIMO, fading) with modified proofs.
- **Boundary behavior:** $R = C$ exactly is the *critical rate* — special achievability results (e.g., sphere-packing) apply but are tight only in special cases.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling of Additive White Gaussian Channels and Continuous-Input Channels with Average Power Constraint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modeling of Additive White Gaussian Channels and Continuous-Input Channels with Average Power Constraint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) Channel

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AWGN Channel Definition:** An **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** channel is a continuous-time, continuous-valued communication channel in which the received signal $Y(t)$ is the sum of the transmitted signal $X(t)$ and an additive noise process $N(t)$ that is both **white** (flat Power Spectral Density across all frequencies) and **Gaussian** (statistically characterized by a Normal/Gaussian probability distribution).

The canonical input–output relationship of the AWGN channel is expressed as:

$$Y(t) = X(t) + N(t)$$

where:
- $X(t)$ = transmitted information-bearing signal (continuous-amplitude, continuous-time)
- $N(t)$ = Additive White Gaussian Noise process with zero mean and Power Spectral Density $\frac{N_0}{2}$
- $Y(t)$ = received signal at the channel output

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Gallery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine you are whispering a secret message to a friend across a **noisy cafeteria**. The original whisper is your signal $X(t)$. The background chatter, clatter of plates, and hiss of the AC together form an *additive* layer of disturbance $N(t)$ that gets *added* to your whisper. Your friend hears $Y(t)$ = (your whisper) + (cafeteria noise). If the cafeteria is uniformly loud at *all pitches* (low, mid, high — like white light contains all colors), the noise is **white**. If the loudness variations follow a **bell-curve pattern** (small disturbances most of the time, occasional huge spikes), the noise is **Gaussian**. This is exactly how the AWGN channel corrupts our communication signals.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The defining quantitative parameters of an AWGN channel are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Average Signal Power** | **P** or **S** | **Watts (W)** | Constraint on input energy |
| **Noise Power Spectral Density** | **$N_0/2$** | **W/Hz** | Two-sided flat PSD |
| **One-sided PSD** | **$N_0$** | **W/Hz** | Positive frequencies only |
| **Noise Variance** | **$\sigma_N^2 = N_0 B$** | **Watts** | Over bandwidth $B$ |
| **Bandwidth** | **B** | **Hz** | Channel passband width |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | **SNR = $S/N$** | **Dimensionless (dB)** | Quality metric |

### Continuous-Input, Continuous-Output (CC) Channel with Average Power Constraint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A continuous-input channel is mathematically defined by the conditional probability density function (PDF) $p(y \mid x)$, where both $X$ (input alphabet) and $Y$ (output alphabet) are continuous sets (typically $\mathbb{R}$). The **average power constraint** restricts the input signal such that $E[X^2] \leq P$ for some permissible power budget $P > 0$.

This constraint models the **physical reality** that every transmitter has a finite energy budget — be it a battery-limited IoT sensor, a satellite with solar panels, or a cellular handset limited by FCC/SAR regulations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the AWGN Model — Component-by-Component Breakdown

### (a) The "Additive" Property

Additivity means the noise is **linearly superimposed** onto the signal. There is no multiplicative distortion, no signal-dependent interference, and no non-linear mixing. Mathematically:

$$Y = X + N \quad \text{with} \quad X \perp N$$

The **statistical independence** ($\perp$) between signal and noise is a foundational assumption. If $X$ and $N$ were correlated, the analysis would shift to compound-Gaussian or interference-channel models.

### (b) The "White" Property — Flat Power Spectral Density

A noise process $N(t)$ is termed **white** if its Power Spectral Density (PSD) is **uniform (constant) across all frequencies** from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ Hz.

$$S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2} \quad \text{for all} \quad f \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

The autocorrelation function is the **Fourier Transform** of the PSD, yielding a Dirac delta function centered at zero lag:

$$R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \delta(\tau)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "White"?** Borrowing from optics — *white light* contains all visible wavelengths in equal proportion. Similarly, white noise contains all frequency components with equal power.

### (c) The "Gaussian" Property — Normal Distribution

At any given time instant $t$, the noise sample $N(t)$ is a Gaussian random variable:

$$N(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_N^2) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$

The probability density function is the classic bell-curve:

$$p_N(n) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_N^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{n^2}{2\sigma_N^2}\right)$$

The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** justifies the Gaussian assumption — when many independent microscopic noise sources (thermal agitation of electrons, shot noise, etc.) superimpose, the aggregate tends toward a Gaussian distribution.

### (d) The Average Power Constraint

The **average power** of the input signal $X$ over the channel symbol interval $T$ is:

$$P_X = E[X^2] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2} X^2(t)\, dt \leq P$$

where $P$ (Watts) is the maximum permissible transmit power. This constraint is **mandatory** because:

- It ensures **physical realizability** (transmitters cannot radiate infinite energy).
- It reflects regulatory limits on radiated power.
- It is the **fundamental budget** that trades off against data rate via Shannon's capacity formula.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Memorize This Table for Module 2 Problems**

| # | Formula / Theorem | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AWGN Channel I/O | $Y = X + N$, with $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_N^2)$ | Baseband signal model |
| 2 | Two-sided PSD of White Noise | $S_N(f) = N_0 / 2$ | Defines noise floor |
| 3 | Autocorrelation of White Noise | $R_N(\tau) = (N_0/2)\delta(\tau)$ | Samples uncorrelated |
| 4 | Noise Power over Bandwidth $B$ | $N = \sigma_N^2 = N_0 B$ | Total integrated noise |
| 5 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = P / (N_0 B)$ | Link quality metric |
| 6 | Differential Entropy (Gaussian, variance $\sigma^2$) | $h(X) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \sigma^2)$ bits/symbol | Foundational for capacity |
| 7 | Mutual Information (CC AWGN) | $I(X;Y) = h(Y) - h(Y \mid X) = h(Y) - h(N)$ | Maximum achievable rate |
| 8 | Capacity-Achieving Input Distribution | $X^* \sim \mathcal{N}(0, P)$ | Optimal signaling is Gaussian |
| 9 | Shannon–Hartley Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ bits/s | **Bandwidth-limited limit** |
| 10 | Capacity (in $nats/s$) | $C = B \ln(1 + P / (N_0 B))$ | Alternate form |
| 11 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = C / B = \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 12 | $E_b/N_0$ Relation | $\text{SNR} = \eta \cdot (E_b / N_0)$ | Energy efficiency tradeoff |
| 13 | Capacity per Unit Bandwidth (Limit) | $\lim_{B \to \infty} C/B = (P / N_0) \log_2 e$ bits/s/Hz | **Power-limited regime** |
| 14 | Bandwidth Expansion Factor | $\beta = B / R$ | Design parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** When entropy uses $\log_2$, capacity is in **bits/s**. When $\ln$ is used, capacity is in **nats/s**. KTU examiners expect you to write the **units explicitly** in answers.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Engineering Domain | AWGN Model Application |
|---|---|
| **Deep-Space Communication (NASA/JPL)** | Signal from Voyager, Cassini — thermal noise dominates, AWGN is the canonical model |
| **Satellite TV (DTH)** | Downlink C-band/Ku-band is AWGN-limited |
| **Fiber-Optic Links** | ASE noise from EDFA amplifiers is well-modeled as AWGN |
| **5G/6G mmWave** | At high SNR, thermal noise floor becomes AWGN-dominant |
| **Radar Signal Processing** | Matched filtering assumes AWGN for optimal detection |
| **Quantum Key Distribution** | Even quantum channels are benchmarked against classical AWGN capacity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Entropy of a Continuous Gaussian Random Variable

This derivation is the cornerstone for computing the AWGN channel capacity.

### Step 1 — State the Definition

For a continuous random variable $X$ with PDF $f(x)$, the **differential entropy** is:

$$h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \log_2 f(x)\, dx \quad \text{(bits)}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian PDF

For $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the PDF is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Take the Logarithm

$$\log_2 f(x) = -\frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi \sigma^2) - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2 \ln 2}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Entropy Integral

$$h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\left[-\frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi \sigma^2) - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2 \ln 2}\right] dx$$

### Step 5 — Split the Integral into Two Parts

$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi \sigma^2) \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx}_{=\,1} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^2 \ln 2} \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x-\mu)^2 f(x)\, dx}_{=\,\sigma^2}$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Two Standard Integrals

The first integral equals **1** by the normalization property of any PDF. The second integral is, by definition, the **variance** $E[(X-\mu)^2] = \sigma^2$.

### Step 7 — Final Compact Result

$$\boxed{\,h(X) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \sigma^2) \quad \text{bits}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The differential entropy depends *only* on the variance $\sigma^2$ — not on the mean $\mu$. Shifting a Gaussian does not change its entropy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capacity of the Continuous AWGN Channel with Average Power Constraint

This is the **flagship derivation** of Module 2 and a guaranteed KTU exam favorite.

### Step 1 — Channel Model and Constraints

Given:
- Input: $X$, continuous-valued
- Output: $Y = X + N$, with $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_N^2)$
- Average power constraint: $E[X^2] \leq P$

### Step 2 — Express the Mutual Information

By the chain rule of mutual information:

$$I(X;Y) = h(Y) - h(Y \mid X)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Conditional Entropy $h(Y \mid X)$

Since $Y = X + N$ and $N$ is independent of $X$, conditioning on $X$ removes the uncertainty about $X$:

$$Y \mid X = x \;\Rightarrow\; Y \mid X = x \sim \mathcal{N}(x, \sigma_N^2)$$

Therefore:

$$h(Y \mid X) = h(N) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \sigma_N^2) \quad \text{bits}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Output Entropy $h(Y)$

The output $Y$ is the sum of two independent random variables: $X$ and $N$. The variance of $Y$ is:

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \text{Var}(Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(N) + 2\,\text{Cov}(X, N)$$

Since $X \perp N$, the covariance term vanishes:

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \sigma_X^2 + \sigma_N^2 \leq P + \sigma_N^2$$

(We used $E[X^2] = \sigma_X^2 + \mu_X^2$, and for zero-mean optimization this equals $\sigma_X^2$.)

The **differential entropy** of $Y$ is maximized when $Y$ is itself Gaussian. But $Y = X + N$ is Gaussian only if $X$ is Gaussian. Hence the optimal input distribution is:

$$X^* \sim \mathcal{N}(0, P)$$

This yields:

$$h(Y) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\big(2\pi e (\sigma_X^2 + \sigma_N^2)\big) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\big(2\pi e (P + \sigma_N^2)\big)$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Mutual Information (Capacity)

$$C = \max_{f_X:\, E[X^2] \leq P} I(X;Y) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\big(2\pi e (P + \sigma_N^2)\big) - \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\big(2\pi e \sigma_N^2\big)$$

### Step 6 — Simplify Using Logarithm Subtraction

Using the identity $\log a - \log b = \log(a/b)$:

$$C = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{P + \sigma_N^2}{\sigma_N^2}\right)$$

### Step 7 — Introduce SNR and Bandwidth

Recall that $\sigma_N^2 = N_0 B$, so the **per-second** capacity (over a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with $2B$ independent samples per second, by Nyquist) is:

$$\boxed{\,C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P}{N_0 B}\right) = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \text{bits/s}\,}$$

This is the celebrated **Shannon–Hartley Theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Capacity-Achieving Distribution Theorem:** For a power-constrained AWGN channel, the input distribution that **maximizes** mutual information is **Gaussian** with zero mean and variance equal to the power constraint. This is provably unique under the constraint $E[X^2] \leq P$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Power-Limited Regime ($B \to \infty$)

### Step 1 — Rewrite Shannon's Capacity

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P}{N_0 B}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $u = N_0 B / P$ and Expand

As $B \to \infty$, the argument of the logarithm approaches 1, motivating a Taylor expansion $\ln(1 + 1/u) \approx 1/u - 1/(2u^2) + \ldots$:

$$C = B \cdot \frac{1}{\ln 2} \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{P}{N_0 B}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Series Expansion for Large $B$

$$\ln\!\left(1 + \frac{P}{N_0 B}\right) \approx \frac{P}{N_0 B} - \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{P}{N_0 B}\right)^{\!2} + \cdots$$

### Step 4 — Take the Limit of Capacity per Hertz

$$\lim_{B \to \infty} \frac{C}{B} = \frac{1}{\ln 2}\cdot \frac{P}{N_0 B} \cdot \text{(unitless adjustment)}$$

After careful evaluation:

$$\boxed{\,\lim_{B \to \infty} \frac{C}{B} = \frac{P}{N_0} \log_2 e \approx 1.4427 \cdot \frac{P}{N_0} \quad \text{bits/s/Hz}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

When bandwidth is **unlimited**, simply widening $B$ does not yield unbounded capacity. The asymptotic ceiling is proportional to $P/N_0$ — the **energy efficiency** dominates.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: $E_b / N_0$ — The Fundamental Energy-Efficiency Metric

### Step 1 — Define Energy per Information Bit

If we transmit $R$ bits/second with power $P$, the energy expended per information bit is:

$$E_b = \frac{P}{R}$$

### Step 2 — Rewrite SNR in Terms of $E_b$

Substituting $P = E_b R$ and $N_0 B = N_0 (R/\eta)$ where $\eta = R/B$ is the spectral efficiency:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P}{N_0 B} = \frac{E_b R}{N_0 \cdot (R/\eta)} = \eta \cdot \frac{E_b}{N_0}$$

### Step 3 — Capacity in $E_b/N_0$ Form

$$\eta = \log_2\!\left(1 + \eta \cdot \frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Minimum $E_b/N_0$

For reliable communication at arbitrarily small bit-error rate, as $\eta \to 0$:

$$2^\eta \approx 1 + \eta \ln 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + \eta \ln 2 \approx 1 + \eta \cdot \frac{E_b}{N_0}$$

Equating the coefficients of $\eta$:

$$\boxed{\,\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\min} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shannon Limit:** No code can transmit reliably below $E_b/N_0 = -1.59$ dB on an AWGN channel. This is the **absolute physical bound** for power-limited AWGN communication.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The AWGN Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source: Message m] --> enc[Channel Encoder: adds redundancy]
    enc --> mod[Modulator: maps bits to waveform X t]
    mod --> tx[Transmitter: Power Amplifier P Watts]
    tx --> ch[AWGN Channel: Y t equals X t plus N t]
    ch --> rx[Receiver: Front End Filter B Hz]
    rx --> demod[Demodulator: soft decisions]
    demod --> dec[Channel Decoder: corrects errors]
    dec --> dst[Destination: estimated message m hat]

    ch -. additive .-> noise[White Gaussian Noise N t with PSD N0 by 2]
    tx -. constraint .-> pwr[Average Power Constraint: E X squared less than or equal to P]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Components of the AWGN Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer["Input Layer"]
        xaxis[Continuous Input X]
        muX[Mean mu sub X]
        sigX[Variance sigma sub X squared]
    end

    subgraph channelLayer["Channel Transformation"]
        addOp[Additive Operation Plus]
        noise[Gaussian N with mean zero and variance sigma N squared]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["Output Layer"]
        yaxis[Continuous Output Y]
        muY[Mean equals mu sub X]
        sigY[Variance equals sigma sub X squared plus sigma N squared]
    end

    subgraph constraintLayer["Design Constraints"]
        pwrConst[Average Power E X squared less than or equal to P]
        bwConst[Bandwidth B Hertz]
        psdConst[Noise PSD equals N0 by 2]
    end

    xaxis --> addOp
    noise --> addOp
    addOp --> yaxis

    pwrConst -. restricts .-> xaxis
    bwConst -. restricts .-> channelLayer
    psdConst -. defines .-> noise

    muX -. affects .-> muY
    sigX -. affects .-> sigY
```

## 4.3 Capacity-vs-SNR Operating Regimes Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Shannon Hartley C equals B log2 1 plus SNR]) --> regCheck{SNR Regime}

    regCheck --> lowSNR[Low SNR Linear Regime]
    regCheck --> highSNR[High SNR Logarithmic Regime]
    regCheck --> bwLimit[Bandwidth Limited Regime]

    lowSNR --> lowF[Capacity grows linearly with SNR: C approx B times SNR over ln 2]
    highSNR --> highF[Capacity grows logarithmically: C approx B log2 SNR]
    bwLimit --> bwF[B is the binding constraint; power is abundant]

    highF --> shannonBound[The Shannon Limit: E sub b over N sub 0 greater than or equal to ln 2 equals minus 1.59 dB]
    lowF --> energyEfficiency[Energy efficiency dominates: P over N sub 0 sets the ceiling]

    shannonBound --> practicalCodes[Modern codes approach within 0.5 dB of limit]
    energyEfficiency --> practicalCodes
```

## 4.4 Spectral Efficiency vs $E_b/N_0$ — The Shannon Bound Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[E sub b over N sub 0 in dB] --> b[Spectral Efficiency eta bits per second per Hertz]
    b --> c[Capacity Achieving Codes]
    c --> d[Turbo Codes LDPC Polar Codes]
    d --> e[Within 0.1 to 0.5 dB of Shannon Limit]
    e --> f[Operating Points for 5G 4G WiFi]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These schematics describe *information flow* rather than physical layout. In the KTU exam, a labeled block diagram with arrows showing $X \to [+] \to Y$ and a side-branch labeled $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ is considered a complete answer for "Sketch the AWGN channel model."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Define an Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) channel. Explain each adjective in the term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The AWGN channel is a continuous-input, continuous-output communication channel whose received signal $Y(t)$ is the sum of the transmitted signal $X(t)$ and an additive noise $N(t)$. The three adjectives carry specific meaning:

- **Additive:** The noise $N(t)$ is added linearly to the signal, i.e., $Y(t) = X(t) + N(t)$, with $N(t)$ statistically independent of $X(t)$.
- **White:** The Power Spectral Density of $N(t)$ is constant across all frequencies: $S_N(f) = N_0/2$, meaning equal power per Hz at every frequency.
- **Gaussian:** At every time instant, $N(t)$ follows the Normal distribution $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_N^2)$, justified physically by the Central Limit Theorem applied to many microscopic noise sources.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **State the Shannon–Hartley theorem and explain the role of the average power constraint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Shannon–Hartley theorem** states that the maximum achievable error-free data rate (channel capacity) over an AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal power $P$ and noise power spectral density $N_0/2$ is:

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P}{N_0 B}\right) \quad \text{bits/s}$$

The **average power constraint** $E[X^2] \leq P$ restricts the input signal energy. Without it, capacity would be infinite. It reflects the physical reality of finite transmitter energy budgets. The constraint dictates that the optimal input distribution is Gaussian with variance $P$, which is the foundation for proving the formula above.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Derive the channel capacity of a continuous-input AWGN channel subject to an average power constraint $E[X^2] \leq P$. Clearly state and justify the optimal input distribution. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A communication system operates over an AWGN channel with bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz and noise power spectral density $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. If the average transmitted power is limited to $P = 10$ mW, compute (i) the channel capacity, (ii) the spectral efficiency, and (iii) the minimum energy per bit $E_b/N_0$ required for error-free transmission. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Model and Constraints:**
Channel: $Y = X + N$, with $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_N^2)$ and constraint $E[X^2] \leq P$.

**Step 2 — Mutual Information Expression:**

$$I(X;Y) = h(Y) - h(Y \mid X)$$

Since $Y \mid X = x$ has the distribution $N(x, \sigma_N^2)$:

$$h(Y \mid X) = h(N) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \sigma_N^2)$$

**Step 3 — Output Entropy:** The maximum of $h(Y)$ subject to $\text{Var}(Y) \leq P + \sigma_N^2$ is achieved when $Y$ is Gaussian, which requires $X$ to be Gaussian.

**Step 4 — Optimal Input:** Set $X^* \sim \mathcal{N}(0, P)$, giving:

$$h(Y) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\big(2\pi e (P + \sigma_N^2)\big)$$

**Step 5 — Capacity:**

$$C = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{P + \sigma_N^2}{\sigma_N^2}\right) = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P}{N_0 B}\right)$$

(The factor $B$ appears when converting to bits/second using Nyquist's $2B$ samples/s.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000$ Hz, $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, hence $N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. $P = 10$ mW $= 10^{-2}$ W.

**(i) Noise Power:**

$$N = N_0 B = (2 \times 10^{-20})(4000) = 8 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W}$$

**Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P}{N} = \frac{10^{-2}}{8 \times 10^{-17}} = 1.25 \times 10^{14}$$

**Channel Capacity:**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \approx 4000 \cdot \log_2(1.25 \times 10^{14})$$

$$\log_2(1.25 \times 10^{14}) = \log_2(1.25) + 14 \log_2(10) \approx 0.3219 + 46.5074 \approx 46.829$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 4000 \times 46.829 \approx 187{,}318 \text{ bits/s} \approx 187.3 \text{ kbps}}$$

**(ii) Spectral Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \approx 46.83 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**(iii) Minimum $E_b / N_0$:**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{\text{SNR}}{\eta} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{14}}{46.83} \approx 2.67 \times 10^{12}$$

In dB: $(E_b/N_0)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.67 \times 10^{12}) \approx 124.27$ dB.

This far exceeds the Shannon limit of $-1.59$ dB — the system is operating deep in the **power-limited regime**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Alternate]**
> **(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the AWGN channel model and the role of the average power constraint. Define the **noise-equivalent bandwidth** and **signal-to-noise ratio**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A power-limited AWGN channel has $P / N_0 = 10^6$ bits/s (in the asymptotic limit $B \to \infty$). Compute (i) the asymptotic spectral efficiency in bits/s/Hz, and (ii) the maximum achievable bandwidth efficiency if the system operates at SNR $= 100$ (20 dB) over $B = 1$ MHz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The AWGN channel is represented as:

$$\boxed{\;X(t) \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{+\;} \;\longrightarrow\; Y(t)\;}$$

with a side branch $N(t)$ entering the summing junction. The **average power constraint** $E[X^2] \leq P$ is shown as an external block limiting the input.

**Average Power Constraint Role (2 marks):**
Without this constraint, the transmitter could radiate arbitrarily large energy, and the capacity would diverge to infinity. The constraint models the **physical, regulatory, and economic limits** on transmission power. It also dictates that the optimal input is Gaussian.

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Noise-equivalent bandwidth $B$:** The bandwidth of an ideal rectangular filter that passes the same total noise power as the actual filter. For a filter with transfer function $H(f)$:

$$B = \frac{1}{\vert H(f_0)\vert^2}\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2 df$$

- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Dimensionless ratio of average signal power to average noise power measured at the receiver input: $\text{SNR} = P / (N_0 B)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Asymptotic Spectral Efficiency (Power-Limited):**

Using the high-bandwidth limit:

$$\eta_\infty = \lim_{B \to \infty} \frac{C}{B} = \frac{P}{N_0} \log_2 e$$

Given $P/N_0 = 10^6$ bits/s:

$$\boxed{\eta_\infty = 10^6 \times 1.4427 \approx 1.4427 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s/Hz}}$$

This is the **energy-limited ceiling** — even with infinite bandwidth, we cannot exceed this spectral efficiency.

**(ii) Maximum Capacity at SNR = 100, $B = 1$ MHz:**

Noise power spectral density from $P/N_0 = 10^6$ implies $N_0 = P / 10^6$. The noise power in 1 MHz is:

$$N = N_0 B = \frac{P \cdot 10^6}{10^6} = P \cdot 1$$

So $\text{SNR} = P/N = 100$ ✓ (consistent).

Channel capacity:

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 10^6 \cdot \log_2(101) \approx 10^6 \times 6.658 \approx 6.658 \text{ Mbps}$$

Bandwidth efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2(101) \approx 6.66 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing bandwidth-limited vs power-limited regimes:** Students often blindly apply Shannon's formula without recognizing the asymptotic regime. When the problem states $B \to \infty$ or $P/N_0$ is fixed, you **must** use the $P/N_0 \log_2 e$ formula — full marks are lost otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $B$ in bits/s:** The formula $C = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + P/N_0 B)$ gives bits per channel use; for bits/second, you **must** multiply by $2B$ (Nyquist rate) or equivalently write $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
> 3. **Units mismatch:** Capacity from $\log_2$ is in **bits/s**; from $\ln$ it is in **nats/s**. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for unlabeled units.
> 4. **Forgetting to justify why Gaussian input is optimal:** A common error is to *state* $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, P)$ without invoking the maximum entropy principle or the variance constraint. Always show that $\text{Var}(X) + \sigma_N^2$ is maximized under $E[X^2] \leq P$, forcing $X$ to be Gaussian.
> 5. **Mixing one-sided and two-sided PSD:** $S_N(f) = N_0/2$ is two-sided; positive-frequency integrals use $N_0$. KTU 2024 papers explicitly check this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2 / AWGN**

- [ ] **AWGN Channel I/O Equation:** Always start with $Y = X + N$, where $X \perp N$ and $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_N^2)$.
- [ ] **Three Adjectives, Three Constraints:** Additive (linear superposition), White (flat PSD $N_0/2$), Gaussian (Normal PDF by CLT).
- [ ] **Two-sided PSD:** $S_N(f) = N_0/2$ for $f \in (-\infty, \infty)$; one-sided uses $N_0$.
- [ ] **Noise Power Formula:** $N = \sigma_N^2 = N_0 B$ (integrate PSD over bandwidth).
- [ ] **Average Power Constraint:** $E[X^2] \leq P$ is non-negotiable; it bounds the transmitter.
- [ ] **Capacity-Achieving Input:** Always Gaussian: $X^* \sim \mathcal{N}(0, P)$.
- [ ] **Differential Entropy of Gaussian:** $h(X) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \sigma^2)$ bits.
- [ ] **Shannon–Hartley Theorem:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ bits/s, where $\text{SNR} = P / (N_0 B)$.
- [ ] **Spectral Efficiency:** $\eta = C / B = \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ bits/s/Hz.
- [ ] **Bandwidth-Limited Regime:** High SNR — capacity grows as $B \log_2(\text{SNR})$.
- [ ] **Power-Limited Regime:** $B \to \infty$ — capacity ceiling is $(P/N_0)\log_2 e$ bits/s.
- [ ] **Shannon Limit on $E_b/N_0$:** $\geq \ln 2 \approx -1.59$ dB for any non-zero rate.
- [ ] **Units Discipline:** $\log_2 \to$ bits; $\ln \to$ nats; convert using $\log_2 x = \ln x / \ln 2$.
- [ ] **Justify, Don't Assume:** Always justify *why* Gaussian input is optimal — invoke entropy maximization under a variance constraint.
- [ ] **Graphs to Memorize:** $C$ vs $B$ (saturates), $C$ vs SNR (logarithmic), $\eta$ vs $E_b/N_0$ (Shannon-bound curve approaching $-1.59$ dB).
- [ ] **Real-World Anchors:** Deep-space probes (Voyager, Cassini), satellite TV, fiber-optic EDFA links — all benchmarked against AWGN capacity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Entropy and Differential Entropy of Gaussian Random Variable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Entropy and Differential Entropy of Gaussian Random Variable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Differential Entropy

In the discrete case, the entropy $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log p_i$ measures the average information of a discrete random variable. When $X$ is a **continuous random variable** with probability density function (PDF) $f(x)$, the natural analog is the **Differential Entropy**, defined as:

$$h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \log f(x) \, dx$$

provided the integral converges. Here, the base of the logarithm determines the unit — base **2** gives **bits/sample**, base **e** (natural log) gives **nats/sample**, and base **10** gives **Hartleys/sample**. The unit conversion follows the standard rule: $1 \text{ nat} = \frac{1}{\ln 2} \text{ bits} \approx 1.4427 \text{ bits}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**
> Unlike discrete entropy $H(X)$, the differential entropy $h(X)$ is **NOT** a measure of absolute information content. It can take **negative values** (e.g., for a uniform distribution on $[0, 0.5]$, $h(X) = \log 0.5 = -1$ bit). It measures **relative information** and is most meaningful when comparing two distributions on the same support, or as a building block for mutual information and channel capacity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Thermometer" of Distributions:**
> Imagine a glass of water representing a probability distribution. **Discrete entropy** is like counting the distinct ice cubes (outcomes) and asking how "surprising" the average pick is. **Differential entropy** is like measuring the *smoothness* or *spread* of the water's surface — a sharp, narrow Gaussian spike has *low* differential entropy (highly predictable, "concentrated"), while a flat, wide uniform distribution has *higher* differential entropy (highly uncertain). The Gaussian, despite being "peaked," is the *most entropic* of all continuous distributions with a fixed variance — a counterintuitive but profound result in information theory.

### 1.3 Formal Definition — Gaussian (Normal) Random Variable

A **continuous random variable** $X$ is said to be **Gaussian** (or **Normally distributed**) if its PDF is given by:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}}, \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

where $\mu = E[X]$ is the **mean** and $\sigma^{2} = \text{Var}(X)$ is the **variance**. The differential entropy of $X$ evaluates to a strikingly simple closed form:

$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) \quad \text{(nats per sample)}$$

$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) \quad \text{(bits per sample)}$$

The term $\mathbf{2\pi e \approx 17.079}$ is a universal constant that appears universally in continuous-information results.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need Differential Entropy?

1. **Continuous sources** (e.g., voice, video, sensor signals) cannot be modeled discretely without quantization loss.
2. **Channel capacity** of the **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** channel is derived using $h(X)$ and $h(X|Y)$.
3. **Source coding theorems** for analog sources (rate-distortion theory) are built on $h(X)$.
4. It provides a **unified framework** where the discrete and continuous worlds are bridged via $H(X) = \lim_{\Delta \to 0} [H(X^{\Delta}) + \log \Delta]$, where $X^{\Delta}$ is the quantized version of $X$ with step size $\Delta$.

### 2.2 Fundamental Properties of Differential Entropy

1. **Translation Invariance**: Shifting a random variable by a constant does not change its differential entropy.
$$h(X + c) = h(X), \quad \text{for any constant } c \in \mathbb{R}$$

2. **Scaling Property**: Scaling a random variable by a constant $a$ adds a $\log \vert a \vert$ term.
$$h(aX) = h(X) + \log \vert a \vert$$

3. **Non-negativity Is Not Guaranteed**: Unlike $H(X) \ge 0$ for discrete variables, $h(X)$ can be negative.
$$h(X) \ge -\infty \quad \text{(lower bound)}$$
$$h(X) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e (E[X^{2}] - (E[X])^{2})) \quad \text{(upper bound, achieved by Gaussian)}$$

4. **Conditioning Reduces Entropy** (as in the discrete case):
$$h(X \vert Y) \le h(X)$$

5. **Chain Rule for Differential Entropy**:
$$h(X, Y) = h(X) + h(Y \vert X) = h(Y) + h(X \vert Y)$$

### 2.3 The "Why" of the Gaussian Result

The Gaussian PDF is the **unique distribution that maximizes differential entropy** for a fixed variance $\sigma^{2}$. This is a direct consequence of **Jensen's inequality** applied to the convex function $-\log x$. The result states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Differential Entropy Theorem (KTU 2024 Module 2 High-Yield):**
> Among all continuous random variables $X$ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$, the Gaussian distribution $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ has the **maximum** differential entropy:
> $$h(X) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$$
> with equality **if and only if** $X$ is Gaussian.

**Engineering Implication**: The AWGN channel is the *worst-case* (noisiest) continuous channel for a given noise power, and the Gaussian source is the *hardest* analog source to compress losslessly — both facts are cornerstones of modern digital communication design.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $h(X) = -\int f(x) \log f(x) \, dx$ | Definition of differential entropy | depends on $\log$ base |
| 2 | $h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$ | Differential entropy of Gaussian $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ | nats |
| 3 | $h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$ | Differential entropy in bits | bits/sample |
| 4 | $h(X + c) = h(X)$ | Translation invariance | same as $h(X)$ |
| 5 | $h(aX) = h(X) + \log \vert a \vert$ | Scaling property | same as $h(X)$ |
| 6 | $h(X) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$ | Max-entropy bound (Gaussian) | nats |
| 7 | $f(x) = \frac{1}{\exp(\lambda_{0} + \lambda_{1} x + \lambda_{2} x^{2})}$ | General form of max-entropy distribution with constraints on $E[1], E[X], E[X^{2}]$ | — |
| 8 | $I(X;Y) = h(X) - h(X \vert Y)$ | Mutual information in terms of differential entropy | bits or nats |
| 9 | $C = \frac{1}{2} \log(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Capacity of AWGN channel per channel use | bits/use |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWGN Channel Capacity (Shannon, 1948)**: $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ is derived directly from the differential entropy of the Gaussian noise.
- **Data Compression (Lossy)**: Rate-distortion functions for Gaussian sources yield $R(D) = \frac{1}{2} \log(\sigma^{2}/D)$ for $D < \sigma^{2}$.
- **Signal Processing**: Minimum mean-square error (MMSE) estimation leverages $h(X \vert Y)$ in Bayesian inference.
- **Machine Learning**: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) maximize a lower bound on log-likelihood using the differential entropy of a Gaussian latent space.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Differential Entropy of a Gaussian Random Variable

**Given:** $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ with PDF:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}}$$

**Goal:** Derive $h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$.

**Step 1: Write the definition with natural logarithm (for nats).**

$$h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, \ln f(x) \, dx$$

**Step 2: Substitute $f(x)$ into the logarithm.**

The logarithm of the Gaussian PDF is:

$$\ln f(x) = \ln\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}}\right]$$

Using the logarithm product rule $\ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b$:

$$\ln f(x) = \ln\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}}\right) + \ln\left(e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}}\right)$$

Since $\ln(1/a) = -\ln(a)$ and $\ln(e^{u}) = u$:

$$\ln f(x) = -\ln\left(\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}\right) - \frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$$

Using the power rule $\sqrt{a} = a^{1/2}$, so $\ln \sqrt{a} = \frac{1}{2} \ln a$:

$$\ln f(x) = -\frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) - \frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute back into the entropy integral.**

$$h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) - \frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right] dx$$

Distribute the negative sign:

$$h(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \left[ \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) + \frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right] dx$$

**Step 4: Split into two integrals (linearity of integration).**

$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx + \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) (x - \mu)^{2} \, dx$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the first integral using the normalization property of PDFs.**

A valid PDF must integrate to 1:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$$

So the first term reduces to:

$$I_{1} = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the second integral using the definition of variance.**

The variance of $X$ is:

$$\sigma^{2} = E[(X - \mu)^{2}] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) (x - \mu)^{2} \, dx$$

So the second term reduces to:

$$I_{2} = \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \cdot \sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 7: Combine both terms.**

$$h(X) = I_{1} + I_{2} = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) + \frac{1}{2}$$

Factor out the $\frac{1}{2}$:

$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) + 1 \right]$$

Apply the logarithm identity $a + 1 = a \cdot e$ inside the log? Actually, we use $\ln(a) + 1 = \ln(a) + \ln(e) = \ln(ae)$:

$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2} \cdot e) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 8: Final result.**

$$\boxed{\,h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) \quad \text{nats per sample}\,}$$

To convert to bits, divide by $\ln 2$:

$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) \quad \text{bits per sample}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** If $\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi e}$, then $h(X) = 0$ bits. For $\sigma^{2} < \frac{1}{2\pi e}$, $h(X) < 0$ — perfectly legal! This shows $h(X)$ measures *concentration*, not absolute information.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Proof — Gaussian Maximizes Differential Entropy

**Theorem:** Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with $E[X] = 0$ and $E[X^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$. Then $h(X) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$, with equality iff $f(x)$ is Gaussian.

**Proof using Kullback–Leibler Divergence (KL Divergence):**

**Step 1:** Recall the KL divergence between two PDFs $f$ and $g$ is:

$$D(f \Vert g) = \int f(x) \log \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} \, dx \ge 0$$

with equality iff $f = g$ almost everywhere.

**Step 2:** Rewrite the KL divergence:

$$D(f \Vert g) = \int f(x) \log f(x) \, dx - \int f(x) \log g(x) \, dx = -h(f) - \int f(x) \log g(x) \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Let $g(x)$ be the standard Gaussian PDF with mean 0 and variance $\sigma^{2}$:

$$g(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}} \, e^{-x^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\log g(x) = -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi \sigma^{2}) - \frac{x^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$ into the second integral:

$$\int f(x) \log g(x) \, dx = -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi \sigma^{2}) \int f(x) \, dx - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \int f(x) x^{2} \, dx$$

**Step 5:** Use the constraints $\int f(x) \, dx = 1$ and $\int f(x) x^{2} \, dx = \sigma^{2}$ (since $E[X^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$):

$$\int f(x) \log g(x) \, dx = -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi \sigma^{2}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \cdot \sigma^{2} = -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi \sigma^{2}) - \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Apply $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$ in reverse, noting $\frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \log e$:

$$\int f(x) \log g(x) \, dx = -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 7:** Plug back into the KL divergence expression:

$$D(f \Vert g) = -h(f) - \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) \right] = \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) - h(f)$$

**Step 8:** Since $D(f \Vert g) \ge 0$:

$$\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) - h(f) \ge 0 \implies h(f) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 9:** Equality holds iff $f = g$, i.e., iff $f$ is the Gaussian PDF. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: Differential Entropy of Gaussian — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Random Variable X] --> B[Has PDF f of x]
    B --> C[Define Differential Entropy]
    C --> D["h of X equals minus integral f of x log f of x dx"]
    D --> E[Case 1 Uniform Distribution]
    D --> F[Case 2 Gaussian Distribution]
    D --> G[Case 3 Exponential Distribution]
    F --> H[Gaussian PDF f of x]
    H --> I["mean mu and variance sigma squared"]
    I --> J[Compute h of X]
    J --> K["Step 1 substitute Gaussian PDF into log"]
    K --> L["Step 2 split into two integrals"]
    L --> M["Integral 1 uses PDF normalization equals 1"]
    L --> N["Integral 2 equals variance sigma squared"]
    M --> O[Combine results]
    N --> O
    O --> P["h of X equals half log two pi e sigma squared"]
    P --> Q[Key Result]
    Q --> R[Used in AWGN Channel Capacity]
    Q --> S[Used in Rate Distortion Theory]
    Q --> T[Used in Source Coding Theorem]
    Q --> U[Max Entropy for Fixed Variance]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Analog Source] --> ADC[Sampler and Quantizer]
    ADC --> ENC[Source Encoder]
    ENC --> CHC[Channel Encoder]
    CHC --> MOD[Modulator]
    MOD --> CH[AWGN Channel]

    DEM --> CHD[Channel Decoder]
    CHD --> SRD[Source Decoder]
    SRD --> OUT[Analog Output]

    HX["Differential Entropy h of X"] --> CAP[Channel Capacity C]
    HNOISE["h of N equals half log two pi e sigma squared"] --> CAP
    CAP --> SHANNON["C equals B log 1 plus S over N"]
    SHANNON --> PERF[Shannon Limit Performance Bound]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Entropy Diagram Across Distributions (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Distributions with Fixed Variance sigma squared] --> T2[Uniform on a to b]
    T1 --> T3[Exponential]
    T1 --> T4[Gaussian]
    T2 --> U1["h equals log 2a equals half log 12 sigma squared"]
    T3 --> E1["h equals 1 minus gamma plus log 2 sigma"]
    T4 --> G1["h equals half log 2 pi e sigma squared"]

    G1 --> COMP
    COMP --> RESULT[Gaussian is Maximum]
```
