<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
**Define differential entropy of a continuous random variable. How does it differ from discrete entropy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a continuous random variable $X$ with PDF $f(x)$, the differential entropy is:

$$h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \log f(x) \, dx$$

**Differences from Discrete Entropy $H(X)$:**

| Property | Discrete $H(X)$ | Continuous $h(X)$ |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Definition | $-\sum p_i \log p_i$ | $-\int f(x) \log f(x) \, dx$ |
| Sign | Always $\ge 0$ | Can be negative |
| Interpretation | Absolute info (bits) | Relative info |
| Convergence | Always exists if sum converges | Requires $\int f \log f$ to exist |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mention the convergence condition ($\int f \log f$ must be finite) and the sign difference — these are the two most-tested aspects.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**
**State and prove (sketch) the maximum differential entropy theorem for a fixed-variance continuous random variable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** For any continuous random variable $X$ with $E[X] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^{2}$:

$$h(X) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$$

**Proof Sketch:** Using KL divergence $D(f \Vert g) \ge 0$ between $f$ and the Gaussian $g$, and substituting the mean and variance constraints, we obtain:

$$D(f \Vert g) = \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) - h(f) \ge 0$$

Hence $h(f) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$, with equality iff $f$ is Gaussian. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, usually split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer ONE full question (either OR choice). Sub-parts typically involve derivations (5 marks) + numerical/reasoning (2 marks).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper]

**(a) (7 marks) Derive the differential entropy of a Gaussian random variable $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$. Show all steps clearly.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^{2}}} \, e^{-(x-\mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})}$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the definition:
$$h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \ln f(x) \, dx$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\ln f(x)$:
$$\ln f(x) = -\frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) - \frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute and distribute the negative sign:
$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) \int f(x) \, dx + \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \int f(x)(x-\mu)^{2} \, dx$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply $\int f(x) \, dx = 1$ (normalization):
$$I_{1} = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply $\int f(x)(x-\mu)^{2} \, dx = \sigma^{2}$ (variance definition):
$$I_{2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Combine:
$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi \sigma^{2}) + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) (7 marks) A Gaussian random variable $X$ has zero mean and variance $\sigma^{2} = 4$. Compute (i) the differential entropy in nats, (ii) the differential entropy in bits, and (iii) verify whether the entropy increases or decreases when the random variable is scaled by a factor of 3.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Entropy in nats (2 marks):**
$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(2\pi e \cdot 4) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(8\pi e)$$
$$= \frac{1}{2} [\ln 8 + \ln \pi + \ln e] = \frac{1}{2} [2.0794 + 1.1447 + 1] = \frac{1}{2}(4.2241) \approx 2.112 \text{ nats/sample}$$

**Part (ii) — Entropy in bits (2 marks):**
$$h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(2\pi e \cdot 4) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\ln(8\pi e)}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.112}{0.6931} \approx 3.047 \text{ bits/sample}$$

**Part (iii) — Scaling (3 marks):**
Using $h(aX) = h(X) + \log_{2} \vert a \vert$ with $a = 3$:
$$h(3X) = h(X) + \log_{2} 3 \approx 3.047 + 1.585 = 4.632 \text{ bits/sample}$$

The entropy **increases** by $\log_{2} 3 \approx 1.585$ bits/sample because scaling spreads the distribution over a wider range, increasing uncertainty.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper]

**(a) (7 marks) Prove that among all continuous random variables with a given variance $\sigma^{2}$, the Gaussian distribution has the maximum differential entropy. Use the KL divergence approach.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State KL divergence definition: $D(f \Vert g) = \int f(x) \log \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} dx \ge 0$, equality iff $f = g$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Let $f$ be the unknown PDF with $E[X] = 0$, $E[X^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$, and $g$ the Gaussian with same moments.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Expand:
$$D(f \Vert g) = -h(f) - \int f(x) \log g(x) \, dx$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute $\int f(x) \log g(x) \, dx = -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$ using the moment constraints.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute:
$$D(f \Vert g) = \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2}) - h(f) \ge 0$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude $h(f) \le \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$ with equality iff $f$ is Gaussian. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) (7 marks) A communication channel adds Gaussian noise with $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma^{2} = N_{0}/2$. (i) Compute the noise differential entropy in bits. (ii) If the signal power is $S = 10$ W and bandwidth $B = 1$ Hz, find the channel capacity using Shannon's formula.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Noise entropy (3 marks):**
$$h(N) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}\left(2\pi e \cdot \frac{N_{0}}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(\pi e N_{0}) \text{ bits/sample}$$

For a symbolic answer, the standard form used in textbooks is:
$$h(N) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$$

**Part (ii) — Channel capacity (4 marks):**
Shannon's AWGN capacity formula:
$$C = B \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

With $B = 1$ Hz, $S = 10$ W, $N = N_{0}/2$ (taking $N = 1$ W for example, or $N_{0}B = \sigma^{2}$):
$$C = 1 \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{10}{1}\right) = \log_{2}(11) \approx 3.459 \text{ bits/sec}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls (Examiner's Warning):**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\log_{2}$ vs $\ln$**: If the problem asks for bits, students often compute nats. Always check the unit in the problem.
> 2. **Sign error in $h(aX)$**: Use $\log \vert a \vert$, not $\log a$, when $a$ may be negative.
> 3. **Conflating $H(X)$ and $h(X)$**: Discrete entropy is **always non-negative**; differential entropy **can be negative**.
> 4. **Mixing up the variance formula**: $\sigma^{2} = E[X^{2}] - (E[X])^{2}$ for the general case, but $\int f(x)(x-\mu)^{2} \, dx$ is the simplified form when $E[X] = \mu$.
> 5. **Missing the equality condition** in the max-entropy theorem: equality holds **iff** $X$ is Gaussian — this is a 1-mark item often missed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Differential Entropy & Gaussian Entropy**

- **Definition**: $h(X) = -\int f(x) \log f(x) \, dx$ — applicable to continuous random variables only.
- **Units**: nats (base $e$), bits (base 2), hartleys (base 10). Conversion: $1 \text{ nat} = 1/\ln 2 \approx 1.4427$ bits.
- **Key Result (Gaussian)**: $h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi e \sigma^{2})$ — independent of the mean $\mu$!
- **Sign**: $h(X)$ **can be negative** (e.g., uniform on $[0, 0.5]$). $h(X) = 0$ when $\sigma^{2} = 1/(2\pi e)$.
- **Translation Invariance**: $h(X + c) = h(X)$.
- **Scaling**: $h(aX) = h(X) + \log \vert a \vert$.
- **Maximum Entropy Theorem**: For a given variance $\sigma^{2}$, the Gaussian distribution **maximizes** $h(X)$. Equality holds **iff** the distribution is Gaussian.
- **AWGN Capacity**: $C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$ — the famous Shannon limit, derived using Gaussian differential entropy.
- **Engineering Role**: Differential entropy is the cornerstone of **continuous channel capacity**, **rate-distortion theory**, and **lossy source coding** (e.g., JPEG, MP3 use Gaussian-like models for DCT coefficients).
- **Exam Mantra**: Always state (a) the formula, (b) the units, (c) the sign convention, and (d) the equality condition when answering Gaussian entropy questions.
- **Common Mistakes**: Do not confuse $h(X)$ with $H(X)$; do not forget to convert to bits when required; do not assume $h(X) \ge 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation Between Differential Entropy and Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Relation Between Differential Entropy and Entropy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In KTU 2024 Scheme Information Theory & Coding (PEEVT754), the **relation between differential entropy and discrete entropy** is established through the process of *quantization* of a continuous random variable. Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with probability density function (PDF) $f(x)$. The **differential entropy** $h(X)$ is defined as: $$ h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \log_2 f(x) \, dx \quad \text{(in bits)} $$ If we discretize (quantize) the continuous variable $X$ into bins of width $\Delta$, the resulting **discrete entropy** of the quantized variable $X^{\Delta}$ is related to the differential entropy by the fundamental identity: $$ \boxed{\,H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta\,} $$ Equivalently, the differential entropy can be expressed as: $$ \boxed{\,h(X) = H(X^{\Delta}) + \log_2 \Delta\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This relation is a *two-mark short-answer favorite* and also frequently tested as a 7-mark derivation problem in End Semester Evaluations. Master the derivation of $H(X^{\Delta}) \approx h(X) + \log_2(1/\Delta)$ as $\Delta \to 0$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Measuring Stick" Interpretation** Imagine you have a continuous stream of water flowing through a pipe. You cannot count the exact amount of water (continuous), but you can measure it by placing a **ruler (quantization step $\Delta$)** along the pipe. Every time the water level crosses a mark on the ruler, you record the count. - **The discrete entropy $H(X^{\Delta})$** = the actual number of bits needed to record the measurement readings on the ruler. - **The differential entropy $h(X)$** = the *intrinsic randomness* of the water itself, independent of the ruler. - **The $-\log_2 \Delta$ term** = the *extra bits* you must spend simply because your ruler has a fixed resolution. If you use a **finer ruler** (smaller $\Delta$), you need **more bits** to describe each measurement. As $\Delta \to 0$, the number of bits grows without bound, even if the underlying signal has finite differential entropy. ## 1.3 Why This Relation Matters in Engineering | Aspect | Discrete Entropy $H(X)$ | Differential Entropy $h(X)$ | |---|---|---| | Domain | Discrete / countable | Continuous / uncountable | | Sign | Always $\geq 0$ | **Can be negative** | | Units | bits | bits | | Scale invariance | Yes | No — $h(aX) = h(X) + \log_2 \vert a \vert$ | > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often wrongly assume $h(X) \geq 0$ like discrete entropy. For example, $X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1/2)$ gives $h(X) = -\log_2 2 = -1$ bit. **Differential entropy is not a true measure of randomness** — it is a *relative* measure that depends on the coordinate system (i.e., the value of $\Delta$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Quantization Bridge The link between discrete and continuous entropy is **quantization**. To compute the entropy of a continuous random variable in practice, we must convert it into a discrete form, since computers and communication channels only handle finite symbols. **Step-by-step logical breakdown:** 1. **Partition the real line** into intervals of equal width $\Delta$: $$ B_i = [i\Delta, (i+1)\Delta), \quad i \in \mathbb{Z} $$ 2. **Assign each $x$ in bin $B_i$ to a discrete symbol** $i$. The probability of symbol $i$ is: $$ p_i = \int_{i\Delta}^{(i+1)\Delta} f(x)\, dx \approx f(x_i)\,\Delta $$ where $x_i$ is a representative point in $B_i$ (e.g., the midpoint). 3. **Compute discrete entropy** of the quantized variable: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i $$ 4. **Decompose the log term** using $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_i f(x_i)\Delta \left[\log_2 f(x_i) + \log_2 \Delta\right] $$ 5. **Take the limit** as $\Delta \to 0$: the Riemann sum $\sum_i f(x_i)\Delta [\cdot]$ converges to an integral: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) \xrightarrow{\Delta \to 0} -\int f(x)\log_2 f(x)\,dx \;-\; \log_2\Delta \cdot \underbrace{\int f(x)\,dx}_{=1} $$ 6. **Final relation:** $$ \boxed{\,H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta\,} $$ ## 2.2 Interpretation of Each Term | Symbol | Meaning | Behavior as $\Delta \to 0$ | |---|---|---| | $H(X^{\Delta})$ | Discrete entropy of quantized $X$ | $\to +\infty$ (since more symbols appear) | | $h(X)$ | Differential entropy of $X$ | **Stays finite** | | $-\log_2 \Delta$ | Resolution penalty (bits per sample) | $\to +\infty$ | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility - **Source Coding (A/D Conversion):** The relation tells us that encoding a continuous source at resolution $\Delta$ requires approximately $h(X) + \log_2(1/\Delta)$ bits per sample. The first term is the *source rate*; the second is the *digitization overhead*. - **Communication Channel Design:** Bandwidth-limited channels require knowledge of $h(X)$ to compute the *capacity gap* $C - h(X)$. - **Data Compression:** LZW, JPEG, MP3 algorithms implicitly exploit this trade-off by choosing appropriate quantization step sizes. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU ESE** | Formula | Expression | When to Use | |---|---|---| | Differential Entropy | $h(X) = -\int f(x)\log_2 f(x)\,dx$ | General continuous RV | | Quantization Probability | $p_i \approx f(x_i)\Delta$ | Midpoint rule | | **Fundamental Relation** | $H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta$ | $\star\star\star$ Most tested | | Inverse Relation | $h(X) = \lim_{\Delta \to 0}\left[H(X^{\Delta}) + \log_2 \Delta\right]$ | Derivation problems | | Uniform RV on $[a,b]$ | $h(X) = \log_2(b-a)$ | Quick numerical Q | | Gaussian RV ($\sigma^2$) | $h(X) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \sigma^2)$ | AWGN channel capacity | | Scaling Property | $h(aX) = h(X) + \log_2 \vert a \vert$ | Transformations | | Bits per Sample at Resolution $\Delta$ | $\approx h(X) + \log_2(1/\Delta)$ | Source coding | ## 2.5 Worked Conceptual Example — Why $h(X)$ Can Be Negative Consider $X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1/2)$. Here $f(x) = 2$ for $x \in [0, 1/2]$. $$ h(X) = -\int_0^{1/2} 2 \log_2 2 \, dx = -2 \cdot \log_2 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = -1 \text{ bit} $$ This is **negative**! However, when quantized with bin width $\Delta = 1/4$: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -1 - \log_2(1/4) = -1 + 2 = 1 \text{ bit} \geq 0 \quad \checkmark $$ The negative $h(X)$ simply reflects that the density $f(x) = 2 > 1$ in some regions, making $\log_2 f(x) > 0$. The quantization relation $H = h - \log\Delta$ correctly resolves this by adding the resolution term to obtain a valid non-negative discrete entropy. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Derivation of $H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **derivation that earns full marks** in a KTU 14-mark question. Follow each step carefully. ### Step 1 — Set up the quantization Partition the real line $\mathbb{R}$ into non-overlapping bins of width $\Delta > 0$: $$ B_i = [i\Delta, \; (i+1)\Delta), \quad i \in \mathbb{Z} $$ The continuous random variable $X$ is mapped to a discrete random variable $X^{\Delta}$ by the rule: $$ X^{\Delta} = i \quad \text{whenever} \quad X \in B_i $$ ### Step 2 — Express the probability mass function The probability that $X^{\Delta}$ takes the value $i$ is the integral of the PDF over the bin: $$ p_i = P(X^{\Delta} = i) = \int_{i\Delta}^{(i+1)\Delta} f(x)\, dx $$ By the **Mean Value Theorem for Integrals**, there exists some point $x_i \in B_i$ such that: $$ p_i = f(x_i) \cdot \Delta $$ This is the key approximation used throughout the derivation. ### Step 3 — Write the discrete entropy $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_{i=-\infty}^{\infty} p_i \log_2 p_i $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $p_i = f(x_i)\Delta$ $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_{i=-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_i)\Delta \; \log_2 \left[ f(x_i)\Delta \right] $$ ### Step 5 — Apply the log identity $\log_2(ab) = \log_2 a + \log_2 b$ $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_{i=-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_i)\Delta \left[ \log_2 f(x_i) + \log_2 \Delta \right] $$ ### Step 6 — Split the sum into two parts $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_{i=-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_i)\Delta \, \log_2 f(x_i) \;-\; \log_2 \Delta \cdot \sum_{i=-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_i)\Delta $$ ### Step 7 — Evaluate the second sum (Riemann sum for total probability) $$ \sum_{i=-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_i)\Delta \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1 $$ This is a fundamental property of any valid PDF. ### Step 8 — Recognize the first sum as a Riemann sum for the differential entropy $$ -\sum_{i=-\infty}^{\infty} f(x_i)\Delta \, \log_2 f(x_i) \;\xrightarrow{\Delta \to 0}\; -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \log_2 f(x)\, dx = h(X) $$ ### Step 9 — Combine and take the limit $\Delta \to 0$ $$ \lim_{\Delta \to 0} H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Note that $\log_2 \Delta \to -\infty$ as $\Delta \to 0$ since $\Delta < 1$. This correctly shows that the discrete entropy of a finely quantized continuous source grows without bound, even if the source itself has finite differential entropy. ### Final Result $$ \boxed{\,H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta\,} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style **Problem:** A continuous random variable $X$ has PDF $f(x) = \frac{1}{2}e^{-\vert x \vert/2}$ (the Laplace distribution with $b=2$). The variable is quantized with $\Delta = 0.25$. Compute $H(X^{\Delta})$ given that $h(X) = \log_2(2e) \approx 2.443$ bits. **Solution:** Step 1 — Identify differential entropy from the formula for Laplace distribution: $$ h(X) = \log_2(2eb) = \log_2(2e \cdot 2) = \log_2(4e) $$ Step 2 — Evaluate numerically: $$ h(X) = \log_2(4) + \log_2(e) = 2 + \frac{1}{\ln 2} \approx 2 + 1.4427 = 3.4427 \text{ bits} $$ Step 3 — Apply the fundamental relation with $\Delta = 0.25 = 2^{-2}$: $$ -\log_2 \Delta = -\log_2(2^{-2}) = 2 $$ Step 4 — Compute: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta = 3.4427 - (-2) = 5.4427 \text{ bits} $$ This means we need approximately **5.44 bits per sample** to encode the Laplace-distributed source at quantization resolution $0.25$. ## 3.3 Verification via Direct Calculation (Sanity Check) We can verify by counting bits per sample. With $\Delta = 0.25$, the total number of quantization levels covering a meaningful range (say $\pm 4$) is $N = 32$. So: $$ H_{\max} = \log_2 32 = 5 \text{ bits} $$ Our computed $5.44$ bits exceeds this slightly because the actual support is infinite. The differential entropy contribution $h(X)$ accounts for the unbounded tail, confirming consistency. ## 3.4 Inverse Derivation — Starting from Discrete, Getting Continuous Given the discrete entropy of the quantized source $H(X^{\Delta})$, we can recover $h(X)$: $$ h(X) = H(X^{\Delta}) + \log_2 \Delta = \lim_{\Delta \to 0} \left[ H(X^{\Delta}) + \log_2 \Delta \right] $$ This shows that **$h(X)$ is the limit of $H(X^{\Delta}) + \log_2 \Delta$** as the quantization becomes infinitely fine. The $+\log_2 \Delta$ term cancels the divergence in $H(X^{\Delta})$, leaving a finite residual that depends only on the *shape* of the distribution. ## 3.5 Algebraic Identity Table | Identity | Formula | |---|---| | Log Split | $\log_2(ab) = \log_2 a + \log_2 b$ | | Riemann Sum | $\sum_i f(x_i)\Delta \to \int f(x)\,dx$ | | Total Probability | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ | | Negative $\log$ | $\log_2 \Delta < 0$ for $\Delta \in (0,1)$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Quantization to Entropy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Continuous RV X
with PDF f x] --> B[Quantization
Bin Width Delta] B --> C[Discrete RV X Delta
Symbol i in Bin i Delta] C --> D[Probability p i
approx f xi times Delta] D --> E[Discrete Entropy
H of X Delta equals minus sum p i log p i] E --> F[Log Decomposition
split log f xi times Delta] F --> G[Two Riemann Sums
Term A and Term B] G --> H[Term A converges to h X
Term B converges to log Delta] H --> I[Final Relation
H X Delta equals h X minus log Delta] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:3px style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Entropy Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISC[DISCRETE DOMAIN] D1[Sample Space countable] D2[Probability Mass Function p x] D3[H X equals minus sum p log p] D4[Always non negative] end subgraph CONT[CONTINUOUS DOMAIN] C1[Sample Space uncountable] C2[Probability Density f x] C3[h X equals minus integral f log f dx] C4[Can be negative] end subgraph BRIDGE[QUANTIZATION BRIDGE] Q1[Partition R into bins of width Delta] Q2[p i equals integral over bin of f x dx] Q3[Limiting Relation] Q4[H X Delta equals h X minus log Delta] end D3 --> Q3 C3 --> Q3 Q3 --> Q4 style BRIDGE fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:2px style Q4 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Limiting Behavior Plot ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Delta decreases toward 0] --> L2[Number of quantization levels increases] L2 --> L3[Bits required H X Delta grows like log 1 over Delta] L3 --> L4[h X stays finite] L4 --> L5[Excess bits equals log 1 over Delta go to digitizer overhead] style L1 fill:#ffe4e1,stroke:#dc143c,stroke-width:2px style L4 fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#9370db,stroke-width:2px style L5 fill:#f0fff0,stroke:#2e8b57,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Information-Theoretic Interpretation Table | Component | Symbol | Role | Behavior under $\Delta \to 0$ | |---|---|---|---| | Quantization Width | $\Delta$ | Resolution of A/D converter | Decreases to 0 | | Number of Levels | $N \approx 1/\Delta$ | Discrete alphabet size | Grows to $\infty$ | | Discrete Entropy | $H(X^{\Delta})$ | Actual bits needed | Grows to $\infty$ | | Differential Entropy | $h(X)$ | Shape-only randomness | **Stays finite** | | Penalty Term | $-\log_2 \Delta$ | Resolution overhead | Grows to $\infty$ | > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The quantization process acts as a *bridge* between the discrete and continuous worlds. Differential entropy $h(X)$ captures the *intrinsic* information content of the distribution, while the $-\log_2 \Delta$ term accounts for the *extrinsic* cost of measuring it. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q:** Define differential entropy of a continuous random variable. How is it related to the entropy of a quantized version of the variable? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The differential entropy $h(X)$ of a continuous random variable $X$ with PDF $f(x)$ is defined as: $$ h(X) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \log_2 f(x)\, dx $$ When $X$ is quantized into bins of width $\Delta$, the resulting discrete random variable $X^{\Delta}$ has entropy $H(X^{\Delta})$. The two are related by: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta $$ This shows that the discrete entropy of the quantized source equals the differential entropy plus a penalty term $-\log_2 \Delta$ that accounts for the quantization resolution. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q:** State whether differential entropy can be negative. Justify with an example. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Yes, differential entropy can be **negative**, unlike discrete entropy. **Justification:** Consider $X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1/2)$ with $f(x) = 2$ for $x \in [0, 1/2]$. $$ h(X) = -\int_0^{1/2} 2 \log_2 2 \, dx = -2 \log_2 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = -1 \text{ bit} $$ This is negative because $f(x) = 2 > 1$ in the support region, making $\log_2 f(x) = 1 > 0$, and the negative sign in front makes the integral negative. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Choice 1 (14 Marks) **Q:** Derive the relation between the differential entropy of a continuous random variable and the entropy of its quantized version. Show that as the quantization step $\Delta \to 0$, the discrete entropy grows without bound while the differential entropy remains finite. *(14 marks)* #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1:** Quantize $X$ into bins of width $\Delta$. The probability of the $i$-th bin is: $$ p_i = \int_{i\Delta}^{(i+1)\Delta} f(x)\, dx \approx f(x_i) \Delta $$ **Step 2:** Discrete entropy: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i = -\sum_i f(x_i)\Delta \log_2[f(x_i)\Delta] $$ **Step 3:** Split the log: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\sum_i f(x_i)\Delta \log_2 f(x_i) - \log_2 \Delta \cdot \sum_i f(x_i)\Delta $$ **Step 4:** Apply Riemann sum limits: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = -\int f(x)\log_2 f(x)\,dx - \log_2 \Delta \cdot 1 = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta $$ #### Part (b) — Limiting Behavior (7 marks) **Step 1:** As $\Delta \to 0$, $-\log_2 \Delta \to +\infty$ since $0 < \Delta < 1$. **Step 2:** Therefore: $$ \lim_{\Delta \to 0} H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) + \infty = +\infty $$ The discrete entropy grows without bound because the number of quantization levels $N = 1/\Delta \to \infty$. **Step 3:** The differential entropy $h(X)$ depends only on the *shape* of $f(x)$, not on $\Delta$, so it remains finite. **Step 4:** Interpretation: $H(X^{\Delta}) \approx h(X) + \log_2 N$ where $N = 1/\Delta$ is the number of levels. The $h(X)$ part is the intrinsic randomness; the $\log_2 N$ part is the cost of using a finer alphabet. --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Choice 2 (14 Marks) **Q:** A continuous random variable $X$ uniformly distributed over $[0, 2]$ is quantized with step size $\Delta = 0.5$. Compute: (a) the differential entropy $h(X)$, and (b) the discrete entropy $H(X^{\Delta})$ using the relation $H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta$. Verify that $H(X^{\Delta})$ is non-negative. *(14 marks)* #### Part (a) — Compute $h(X)$ (7 marks) **Step 1:** For $X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 2)$, the PDF is: $$ f(x) = \frac{1}{2-0} = \frac{1}{2}, \quad x \in [0, 2] $$ **Step 2:** Apply the differential entropy formula: $$ h(X) = -\int_0^2 \frac{1}{2} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) dx $$ **Step 3:** Note $\log_2(1/2) = -1$: $$ h(X) = -\int_0^2 \frac{1}{2} \cdot (-1)\, dx = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^2 dx = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = 1 \text{ bit} $$ Alternatively, using the general formula $h(X) = \log_2(b-a)$ for Uniform $[a,b]$: $$ h(X) = \log_2(2-0) = \log_2 2 = 1 \text{ bit} \quad \checkmark $$ #### Part (b) — Compute $H(X^{\Delta})$ and verify (7 marks) **Step 1:** Apply the fundamental relation: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $h(X) = 1$ and $\Delta = 0.5 = 2^{-1}$: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = 1 - \log_2(2^{-1}) = 1 - (-1) = 2 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3:** Verify via direct calculation. With $\Delta = 0.5$ on $[0, 2]$, there are exactly $N = 4$ bins, each equally likely (since uniform distribution): $$ H_{\text{direct}} = \log_2 4 = 2 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 4:** Verification: $$ H(X^{\Delta}) = 2 \text{ bits} \geq 0 \quad \checkmark $$ The relation is consistent, and the discrete entropy is indeed non-negative as expected. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting the base of logarithm:** If $h(X)$ is in nats, the relation becomes $H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \ln \Delta$. KTU typically expects **base-2 (bits)**. Mixing bases = 0 marks for the final answer. > > 2. **Sign errors on $-\log_2 \Delta$:** Students often write $+\log_2 \Delta$ instead of $-\log_2 \Delta$. Since $\Delta < 1$, $\log_2 \Delta < 0$, so $-\log_2 \Delta > 0$. The correct sign in the relation is $-\log_2 \Delta$. > > 3. **Confusing $H(X^{\Delta})$ with $H(X)$:** $H(X^{\Delta})$ is the discrete entropy of the quantized version, **not** the differential entropy. These are fundamentally different quantities. > > 4. **Skipping the Riemann sum justification:** In derivation problems, you must explicitly show the step $\sum_i f(x_i)\Delta \to \int f(x)\,dx$ as $\Delta \to 0$. Skipping this = loss of 2-3 marks. > > 5. **Not stating the unit (bits):** Always write the unit. KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless answers. > > 6. **Forgetting to verify non-negativity:** When asked to compute $H(X^{\Delta})$, always check $H(X^{\Delta}) \geq 0$ as a sanity check. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Relation Between Differential Entropy and Entropy** - **Core formula:** $H(X^{\Delta}) = h(X) - \log_2 \Delta$ ⭐⭐⭐ (most important) - **Differential entropy:** $h(X) = -\int f(x)\log_2 f(x)\,dx$ - **Quantization probability:** $p_i \approx f(x_i)\Delta$ - **Key insight:** $h(X)$ is **independent of $\Delta$**, while $H(X^{\Delta})$ **grows as $-\log_2 \Delta$** - **As $\Delta \to 0$:** $H(X^{\Delta}) \to +\infty$, $h(X)$ stays finite - **Sign property:** $h(X)$ can be negative (e.g., Uniform$(0, 1/2)$ gives $h(X) = -1$ bit) - **Scale invariance fails:** $h(aX) = h(X) + \log_2 \vert a \vert$ - **Uniform RV shortcut:** $h(X) = \log_2(b-a)$ for Uniform on $[a,b]$ - **Engineering meaning:** Bits per sample at resolution $\Delta$ = $h(X) + \log_2(1/\Delta)$ - **Discrete entropy is always $\geq 0$**, but differential entropy is not. - **Memorize the derivation sequence:** (1) Quantize, (2) Write $p_i$, (3) Compute $H$, (4) Split log, (5) Take Riemann sum limit, (6) Get final relation. - **Common KTU trap:** Mixing log bases (bits vs nats) — always check!

topics.title: Shannon-Hartley Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT754 - INFORMATION THEORY & CODING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shannon-Hartley Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** is a landmark result in information theory, established by **Claude Shannon (1948)** and independently by **Ralph Hartley**, which states the maximum achievable rate of error-free information transmission (the **channel capacity**, $C$) over a communications channel of finite **bandwidth** $B$ (in Hertz) that is corrupted by **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)**. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits per second, bps]}$$

where:
- $C$ = Channel capacity (bits/second)
- $B$ = Channel bandwidth (Hz)
- $S$ = Average signal power (Watts)
- $N$ = Average noise power (Watts)
- $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR, dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Shannon-Hartley theorem defines the *theoretical upper bound* on the data rate that can be transmitted over a band-limited channel with additive white Gaussian noise. This is a foundational result for digital communication system design and is **mandatory** for the 14-mark ESE questions.

> [!NOTE]
> The theorem is **channel-specific** to AWGN channels. It assumes the noise is Gaussian-distributed, white (flat power spectral density), and additive. The capacity $C$ is achieved only when the signaling scheme is **optimally coded**; practical systems always operate at rates $R < C$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **highway tunnel** 🚗:

- The **width of the tunnel** = **Bandwidth ($B$)** — wider tunnels allow more lanes (more cars per unit time).
- The **headroom inside the tunnel** = **Signal Power ($S$)** — more headroom lets you drive faster and carry bigger trucks (more information per car).
- The **fog inside the tunnel** = **Noise Power ($N$)** — fog obscures vision, forcing drivers to slow down and creating risk of accidents (bit errors).

The Shannon-Hartley theorem is like a **traffic engineer's master rule**: it tells you the **absolute maximum number of cars (bits) that can pass safely per second** through a tunnel of given width and fog density. You can *theoretically* drive at this rate if you drive perfectly (perfect coding), but practically you must drive a bit slower to avoid accidents (error margin).

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Channel Capacity | **bits/second (bps)** | Upper bound on error-free rate |
| $B$ | Bandwidth | **Hertz (Hz)** | Frequency range of channel |
| $S$ | Signal Power | **Watts (W)** | Average transmitted power |
| $N$ | Noise Power | **Watts (W)** | AWGN over bandwidth $B$ |
| $N_0$ | Noise PSD | **W/Hz** | $N = N_0 \cdot B$ |
| $S/N$ | SNR (linear) | dimensionless | Often expressed in dB |
| $E_b$ | Energy per bit | **Joules (J)** | $E_b = S/C$ |
| $E_b/N_0$ | Bit-energy to noise-density | dimensionless | Key metric in digital comm. |

> [!TIP]
> **Useful conversions:**
> $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$
> $$N = N_0 \cdot B$$
> $$E_b = \frac{S}{C} = \frac{S}{B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Theorem

The Shannon-Hartley theorem has three key operational pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Bandwidth-Limited Regime
- As $B \to \infty$ with fixed $S/N$, the capacity grows only **logarithmically**.
- A doubling of bandwidth **does NOT** double the capacity; it adds a fixed increment.
- This is the basis for **spread-spectrum** systems and why **unbounded bandwidth cannot give infinite capacity** at fixed power.

### Pillar 2 — Power-Limited Regime
- For a fixed bandwidth $B$, capacity grows as $\log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- In the **high-SNR** limit, $C \approx B \cdot \log_2(S/N)$ — capacity increases **only logarithmically** with power.
- In the **low-SNR** limit ($S/N \ll 1$), $C \approx B \cdot (S/N) / \ln 2$ — capacity is **linear** in power and bandwidth.

### Pillar 3 — Bandwidth–Power Trade-off
- The theorem implies a fundamental **trade-off**: one can exchange bandwidth for power (and vice versa) to maintain a target capacity.
- Systems with **ample bandwidth but low power** (e.g., satellite downlinks) are **power-limited**.
- Systems with **strict bandwidth but ample power** (e.g., coaxial cable) are **bandwidth-limited**.

## 2.2 Underlying Assumptions

1. The channel is **memoryless** AWGN.
2. The transmitted signal has an **average power constraint** $\leq S$.
3. The noise has **two-sided power spectral density** $N_0/2$ over the entire band.
4. The total noise power integrated over bandwidth is $N = N_0 \cdot B$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essential and must be memorized.**

| # | Formula | Description | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | **Shannon-Hartley capacity** | AWGN channel |
| 2 | $N = N_0 \cdot B$ | Noise power from PSD | Thermal/AWGN |
| 3 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | SNR in decibels | Log conversion |
| 4 | $C/B = \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | **Spectral efficiency** (bps/Hz) | Normalized capacity |
| 5 | $E_b = S/C$ | Energy per bit | Power-rate ratio |
| 6 | $E_b/N_0 = (S/N) \cdot (1/C) \cdot B$ | Bit-energy-to-noise ratio | Reliability metric |
| 7 | $E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{S/N}{C/B} = \dfrac{S/N}{\log_2(1 + S/N)}$ | **Fundamental $E_b/N_0$ vs SNR relation** | Capacity bound |
| 8 | $C_\infty \to \infty$ as $B \to \infty$ | Unbounded $B$ gives infinite $C$ only if power scales | Conceptual |
| 9 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{\text{linear}})$ | Alternative form using linear SNR | Numerical |
| 10 | $\dfrac{C}{B} \leq \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N_0 B}\right)$ | Capacity per Hz form | Spectral |

## 2.4 The $E_b/N_0$ vs. Bandwidth-Efficiency Trade-off

A very commonly tested KTU concept is the **relationship between $E_b/N_0$ and spectral efficiency** $\eta = C/B$:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S/N}{\log_2(1 + S/N)} = \frac{2^{\eta} - 1}{\eta}$$

This shows that for a given required bit-error-rate, increasing spectral efficiency $\eta$ demands a higher $E_b/N_0$. The **absolute minimum** $E_b/N_0$ is approached as $\eta \to 0$ (bandwidth $\to \infty$):

$$\lim_{\eta \to 0} \frac{E_b}{N_0} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \quad (-1.59 \text{ dB})$$

This famous number is called the **Shannon limit** — the absolute minimum energy per bit required to communicate reliably.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **5G/4G cellular networks** use the Shannon limit to determine cell-edge coverage and link budget.
> - **Satellite communications** operate in the power-limited regime and approach Shannon capacity using **Turbo codes** and **LDPC codes**.
> - **DSL broadband** is bandwidth-limited and uses **DMT (Discrete Multi-Tone)** to allocate bits per sub-channel according to per-tone SNR — a direct application of Shannon's water-filling principle.
> - **Optical fiber systems** are noise-limited at high bit rates, and Shannon-Hartley governs the fundamental limit of long-haul links.

## 2.5 The Shannon Limit in dB

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\min} = \ln(2) \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Students often confuse $\ln 2$ with $\log_{10} 2$. Always use natural log when deriving the limit, then convert: $10 \log_{10}(\ln 2) \approx -1.5917$ dB.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shannon-Hartley Capacity (Intuitive)

Although the rigorous derivation uses information-theoretic arguments (jointly typical sequences, channel coding theorem), the **intuitive form** can be motivated as follows:

**Starting point:** A signal of power $S$ and bandwidth $B$ Hz can be resolved into $2B$ independent samples per second (Nyquist sampling theorem).

**Step 1 — M-ary signaling capacity:**
With $M$ distinguishable amplitude levels and Nyquist sampling at $2B$ samples/sec, the symbol rate is $2B$ symbols/sec. Each symbol carries $\log_2 M$ bits, so:

$$C_{\text{nyquist}} = 2B \cdot \log_2 M \quad \text{[bps]}$$

**Step 2 — Power constraint:**
The minimum spacing between $M$ amplitude levels in the presence of noise of standard deviation $\sigma$ is approximately $2\sigma$. For uniform levels spanning the dynamic range $\pm A$, we have $M \approx A/\sigma$.

**Step 3 — Express $A$ in terms of $S$:**
The average power is $S = A^2/3$ for a uniform distribution, so $A = \sqrt{3S}$.

**Step 4 — Express $\sigma$ in terms of $N$:**
The noise variance is $\sigma^2 = N$, so $\sigma = \sqrt{N}$.

**Step 5 — Combine:**
$$M \approx \frac{\sqrt{3S}}{\sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{3 \cdot \frac{S}{N}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into capacity:**
$$C \approx 2B \cdot \log_2\left(\sqrt{3 \cdot \frac{S}{N}}\right) = B \cdot \log_2\left(3 \cdot \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **3** depends on the assumed distribution of levels. Shannon showed that the *true* maximum over all possible input distributions is achieved by a **Gaussian input**, which gives the elegant form $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — without the 3.

**Step 7 — Shannon's rigorous result:**
By optimizing the input distribution (Gaussian for power-constrained AWGN), Shannon proved:

$$\boxed{C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: From $C$ to $E_b/N_0$ in Terms of Spectral Efficiency

This is a **frequently tested KTU derivation** (worth 5–7 marks).

**Given:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.

**Step 1:** Define spectral efficiency $\eta \triangleq C/B$ (in bps/Hz).

$$\eta = \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $S/N$:

$$2^{\eta} = 1 + S/N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S/N = 2^{\eta} - 1$$

**Step 3:** Express $E_b$ in terms of $S$ and $C$:

$$E_b = \frac{S}{C} = \frac{S}{\eta \cdot B}$$

**Step 4:** Express $N$ in terms of $N_0$ and $B$:

$$N = N_0 \cdot B$$

**Step 5:** Form the ratio $E_b / N_0$:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S}{\eta B} \cdot \frac{B}{N_0} = \frac{S}{N_0 \cdot \eta} = \frac{S/N}{\eta}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $S/N = 2^{\eta} - 1$:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2^{\eta} - 1}{\eta}$$

**Step 7 (limiting case):** As $\eta \to 0$, apply L'Hôpital's rule:

$$\lim_{\eta \to 0} \frac{2^{\eta} - 1}{\eta} = \lim_{\eta \to 0} \frac{2^{\eta} \ln 2}{1} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

In decibels:

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\min} = 10 \log_{10}(\ln 2) \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Shannon limit** — the absolute minimum energy per bit per noise Hz required for *any* arbitrarily reliable communication on the AWGN channel. No code, no modulation, no matter how clever, can beat this bound.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A communication channel has $B = 4$ kHz bandwidth, signal power $S = 10$ mW, and noise power $N = 1$ mW. Find (a) channel capacity, (b) spectral efficiency, (c) $E_b/N_0$ in dB, (d) the minimum $E_b/N_0$ for reliable communication.

**Step (a) — Capacity:**

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 10) = 4000 \cdot \log_2(11)$$

Compute $\log_2(11)$:

$$\log_2(11) = \frac{\ln 11}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.3979}{0.6931} \approx 3.4594$$

Therefore:

$$C = 4000 \cdot 3.4594 = 13{,}837.6 \text{ bps} \approx 13.84 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step (b) — Spectral efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{C}{B} = 3.4594 \text{ bps/Hz}$$

**Step (c) — $E_b/N_0$ in dB:**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S/N}{\eta} = \frac{10}{3.4594} \approx 2.8907 \quad (\text{linear})$$

$$10 \log_{10}(2.8907) \approx 4.61 \text{ dB}$$

**Step (d) — Shannon limit:**

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\min} = 10 \log_{10}(\ln 2) \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}$$

Since $4.61$ dB $> -1.59$ dB, **reliable communication is possible** with a margin of $\approx 6.2$ dB.

## 3.4 Capacity Comparison Table (Common KTU Sub-Question)

| Bandwidth $B$ | SNR $S/N$ | SNR (dB) | Capacity $C$ (bps) | Spectral Eff. (bps/Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3 kHz | 100 | 20 dB | 19,974 | 6.66 |
| 3 kHz | 10 | 10 dB | 10,388 | 3.46 |
| 3 kHz | 1 | 0 dB | 3,000 | 1.00 |
| 3 kHz | 0.1 | -10 dB | 434 | 0.145 |
| 1 MHz | 1000 | 30 dB | 9.97 Mbps | 9.97 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Communication System with Capacity Bound

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of a communication system and the position of the Shannon-Hartley capacity bound within it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Information Source] --> encA[Source Encoder]
    encA --> chEncA[Channel Encoder]
    chEncA --> modA[Modulator]
    modA --> chanA[AWGN Channel B Hz, Noise N0]
    chanA --> demodA[Demodulator]
    demodA --> chDecA[Channel Decoder]
    chDecA --> srcDecA[Source Decoder]
    srcDecA --> destA[Destination]

    chanA -.-> capA[Shannon Hartley Capacity C equals B log2 of 1 plus S over N]
    capA -.-> metricA[Spectral Efficiency C over B]
    capA -.-> metricB[Bit Energy E_b over N_0]
    metricB -.-> limitA[Shannon Limit ln 2 equals minus 1.59 dB]

    style srcA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    style destA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    style capA fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    style limitA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38f00,color:#000
    style chanA fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#00802b,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Trade-off Diagram — Bandwidth vs Power

The Shannon-Hartley theorem implies a **fundamental trade-off** between bandwidth and power for a target capacity. The Mermaid diagram below illustrates this trade-off.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    goalA[Target Capacity C required] --> powerLim[Power Limited Regime]
    goalA --> bwLim[Bandwidth Limited Regime]

    powerLim --> pNote[Low S, ample B. Increase B to compensate. Example: Satellite downlink]
    bwLim --> bNote[Low B, ample S. Increase S to compensate. Example: Coaxial cable modem]

    pNote --> eqP[Increase B to raise C logarithmically]
    bNote --> eqB[Increase S over N to raise C logarithmically]

    eqP --> waterFill[Water Filling Solution: allocate power across frequency]
    eqB --> waterFill

    waterFill --> capCompute[Shannon Hartley Formula Gives the Achievable Bound]

    style goalA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    style powerLim fill:#ffe0cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style bwLim fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    style waterFill fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#00802b,color:#000
    style capCompute fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Capacity as a Function of SNR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA[SNR S over N in dB] --> lowSNR[SNR less than 0 dB]
    axisA --> medSNR[SNR 0 to 20 dB]
    axisA --> highSNR[SNR greater than 20 dB]

    lowSNR --> lowMsg[Capacity C is linear in S over N, dominated by noise floor]
    medSNR --> medMsg[Capacity C is logarithmic in S over N, design region for most systems]
    highSNR --> highMsg[Capacity C grows slowly, power inefficient. Diminishing returns begin]

    medMsg --> designP[Design Sweet Spot: Match Modulation and Coding to Operating Point]

    style lowSNR fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#00802b,color:#000
    style medSNR fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38f00,color:#000
    style highSNR fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    style designP fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
```
